On large screens, it shares the same 6–7-column width and right alignment as the list. There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the last package item and the top of the form, visually signaling a transition from browsing to taking action. Within the form, controls (such as selected package summary, quantity, and a prominent “Add to Cart with Coupon” button) are left-aligned inside the form container with 16–24px padding and 8–16px spacing between controls. The primary button is aligned towards the bottom-right of this form block to match the F-pattern emphasis. On smaller screens, the form spans full-width and appears after the packages list, with enough vertical spacing (24–32px) to separate it as a distinct call-to-action section. If the form is tied to per-package controls, it appears as a consolidated section summarizing the user’s choice rather than repeating under each list item, keeping the page uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block placed at the end of the right column’s flow. It scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick to the viewport, although on very short lists it may appear within the first viewport alongside the lower portion of the left column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-result-banner appears as a full-width notification bar within the main content container, directly below the hero and coupon-category-navigation and above the overlapping coupon-summary-panel and right column. It spans the full 12-column content width, with 16–24px vertical padding and a background color that contrasts with the page (e.g., a soft green for success or red/orange for errors) while staying within the existing brand palette. When visible, it pushes the overlapping section slightly downward, maintaining a 16–24px gap between the bottom edge of the banner and the top edge of the coupon-summary-panel, so the overlapping effect relative to the hero remains readable. On smaller screens, the banner remains full-width and sits in the same relative position—immediately under the hero—ensuring that feedback is seen before the user scrolls back into detailed content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the flow but conditionally rendered. When shown, it occupies vertical space and shifts subsequent components downward; when hidden, no space is reserved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The coupon-category-navigation is positioned just below the hero section’s main heading, within the same content-width container that holds the overlapping panels. It appears as a horizontal row of text links or pill-style buttons stretching across the central 12-column grid, typically centered vertically in a slim bar 40–56px tall. On large screens, links are arranged in a single row, left-aligned or center-aligned within the content width, with 16–24px horizontal spacing between items to encourage a quick left-to-right scan. A 16–24px gap separates this navigation row from the bottom of the hero image, and another 16–24px gap separates it from the top edge of the overlapping coupon-summary-panel. Visually, it acts as a sub-navigation just under the big page title, reinforcing the F-pattern across the top. On smaller screens, the links may wrap to a second row but remain within the same horizontal band, with adequate vertical spacing (8–12px) between lines.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline navigation bar that sits between the hero and main overlapping content. It does not stick on scroll and simply moves up as the user scrolls down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component has no direct visual representation. It operates behind the scenes, ensuring that the current coupon selection, applied filters in the applicable-services-selector, and any selection state in the eligible-service-packages-list are reflected in the URL (and vice versa). Its presence does not alter the layout, spacing, or visual hierarchy of the page. Any changes it triggers are reflected only through other components updating their visual state within their existing layout positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, stateful behavior only. It does not occupy space in the layout and does not affect component positioning, functioning purely as a synchronization layer tied to the browser’s URL and history.

name: Maintenance Plans
title: Maintenance Plans
description: Page to select a vehicle by year/make/model, retrieve applicable maintenance plans, and allow users to view plan details or select multiple plans for comparison.

core_features: Vehicle selection form with cascading dropdowns for year, make, and model using getVehicleYears, getVehicleMakes, and getVehicleModels.
Maintenance plan search that retrieves plans for the selected vehicle using getMaintenancePlansForVehicle.
Results list displaying key maintenance plan attributes, including monthly price, warranty duration, and billing cycle.
Navigation to a plan detail page when a user clicks on a plan name or a dedicated details action.
Plan comparison selection via checkboxes, with a Compare Selected button that navigates to the comparison page when at least two plans are selected.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load with no URL parameters
1. User opens maintenance_plans.html without query parameters.
2. Page initializes the vehicle selector form.
3. The vehicle selector component calls getVehicleYears to populate the year dropdown.
4. Make and model dropdowns are initially disabled or empty until prerequisites are selected.
5. User selects a year; the component calls getVehicleMakes(year) to populate the make dropdown.
6. User selects a make; the component calls getVehicleModels(year, make) to populate the model dropdown.
7. User selects a model and submits the form.
8. The page updates the URL to include vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel parameters.
9. The plan results component calls getMaintenancePlansForVehicle(vehicleYear, vehicleMake, vehicleModel) and renders the returned plans.

Workflow: Page load with vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel in the URL
1. User lands on maintenance_plans.html with vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel query parameters.
2. URL state manager reads these parameters and initializes the vehicle selector form with the given values.
3. The vehicle selector component calls getVehicleYears and sets the selected year from the URL if it exists in the returned list.
4. When a valid year is set, the component calls getVehicleMakes(year); after makes are loaded, it selects the make from the URL if present.
5. When a valid make is set, the component calls getVehicleModels(year, make); after models are loaded, it selects the model from the URL if present.
6. Once year, make, and model are all valid, the plan results component automatically calls getMaintenancePlansForVehicle(vehicleYear, vehicleMake, vehicleModel).
7. Plans are displayed without requiring the user to resubmit the form.

Workflow: Selecting plans for comparison and navigating to comparison page
1. After plans are loaded, each plan row includes a checkbox for comparison selection.
2. User checks one or more plan checkboxes; the comparison selector component updates its internal list of selected plan IDs and synchronizes this list into the URL (for example, as selectedPlanIds).
3. While fewer than two plans are selected, the Compare Selected button remains disabled.
4. When at least two plans are selected, the Compare Selected button becomes enabled.
5. User clicks the enabled Compare Selected button.
6. The component reads the currently selected plan IDs and composes a comma-separated string.
7. The browser navigates to plan_compare.html with planIds set to the comma-separated list in the query string.

Workflow: Viewing plan details
1. In the plan results list, each plan name and a dedicated details action are rendered as navigable elements.
2. User clicks on a plan name or the details action for that plan.
3. The click handler retrieves the relevant plan ID from the bound data record (using the plan_id property returned by getMaintenancePlansForVehicle).
4. The browser navigates to plan_detail.html with planId set to the selected plan ID in the query string.
interactions: On page load, the URL state manager reads query parameters (vehicleYear, vehicleMake, vehicleModel, and optional selectedPlanIds) and initializes component state accordingly.
On vehicle year dropdown focus or change, the vehicle selector validates the current year and triggers getVehicleMakes(year) when a new valid year is chosen.
On vehicle make dropdown change, the vehicle selector validates the current make and triggers getVehicleModels(year, make) when a new valid make is chosen.
On vehicle model dropdown change, the vehicle selector updates internal state; if all three fields are valid and the user submits the form, it triggers plan retrieval.

On vehicle selection form submit, the page prevents default form submission behavior, updates the URL with the selected vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel, and calls getMaintenancePlansForVehicle using these values.

On successful response from getMaintenancePlansForVehicle, the plan results component replaces any existing results with the new list and resets comparison selections that refer to plans no longer present.
If getMaintenancePlansForVehicle returns an empty array, the plan results component displays a no-results state instead of plan items.

On each plan row, clicking the checkbox toggles that plan’s presence in the comparison selection set; the comparison selector recalculates the number of selected plans and enables or disables the Compare Selected button accordingly.

If a user attempts to click Compare Selected when the button is disabled (for example by keyboard focus), the click handler performs no navigation and may present a validation message indicating that at least two plans must be selected.

Clicking Compare Selected when enabled causes the component to collect all selected plan IDs, sort them consistently if needed for stable URLs, join them into a comma-separated string, and navigate to plan_compare.html with the planIds query parameter set to that string.
Clicking a plan name or dedicated details action in a plan row reads the plan_id from that row’s data and navigates to plan_detail.html with planId set to that value.

When URL parameters change due to navigation within the page (for example, the user selects a different vehicle and resubmits the form), components listening to history or URL changes update their state accordingly so that the UI remains in sync with the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL query parameters and in-memory component state derived from them. The key URL parameters are: vehicleYear (numeric value representing the selected vehicle year), vehicleMake (string representing the selected make), vehicleModel (string representing the selected model), and selectedPlanIds (optional comma-separated list of plan IDs currently marked for comparison on this page). On initial load, the URL state manager parses these parameters. If vehicleYear is present and valid, it initializes the year dropdown to that value; otherwise, the year dropdown starts with no selection. After getVehicleYears populates the year dropdown, the component checks whether the URL-provided year exists in the returned list; if not, it clears the year selection and removes vehicleYear from the URL when the user next submits or updates the form. The same pattern applies to vehicleMake and vehicleModel, which are only considered valid after the corresponding getVehicleMakes and getVehicleModels calls succeed and the values are found in their respective lists. When the user submits the vehicle selection form, the page updates (or adds) vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel in the query string before calling getMaintenancePlansForVehicle. If all three parameters are present and valid on page load, getMaintenancePlansForVehicle is called automatically without additional user input. The selectedPlanIds parameter reflects the current set of plans selected for comparison; each time the user toggles a plan's comparison checkbox, the comparison selector computes the new set of selected plan IDs and updates selectedPlanIds in the URL to a comma-separated string representation. If the selection becomes empty, the parameter can be removed from the URL. Changes to selectedPlanIds do not trigger additional API calls; they only affect UI state and the behavior of the Compare Selected button. Navigation to plan_compare.html and plan_detail.html uses query parameters only; plan_compare.html is opened with planIds set to the comma-separated list of selected plan IDs, and plan_detail.html is opened with planId set to the clicked plan's ID. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; reloading the page reconstructs state solely from the URL and fresh API responses.

components.functionality: Manages the vehicle selection workflow via year, make, and model dropdowns, drives the maintenance plan search by calling getVehicleYears, getVehicleMakes, and getVehicleModels, and triggers getMaintenancePlansForVehicle when the user submits a complete vehicle selection.
event_handlers: onInitLoadYears: called on component initialization; invokes getVehicleYears and populates the year dropdown. After years are loaded, it checks URL parameters and preselects the year if present and valid.

onChangeYear: triggered when the user selects or changes the year dropdown; clears make and model selections, updates the URL's vehicleYear (removing vehicleMake and vehicleModel if necessary), and calls getVehicleMakes(year) to populate the make dropdown.

onChangeMake: triggered when the user selects or changes the make dropdown; clears the model selection, updates vehicleMake in the URL, and calls getVehicleModels(year, make) to populate the model dropdown.
onChangeModel: triggered when the user selects or changes the model dropdown; updates vehicleModel in the URL in memory (actual URL update can be finalized on submit depending on desired UX).

onSubmitVehicleSelection: triggered when the user submits the vehicle selection form; validates that year, make, and model are all present and valid; writes or updates vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel in the URL; and notifies the plan results component to call getMaintenancePlansForVehicle using the selected values.

components.functionality: Retrieves and displays the list of maintenance plans for the selected vehicle by calling getMaintenancePlansForVehicle with the current vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel. Shows key attributes for each plan and exposes actions to view details or select the plan for comparison.
event_handlers: onVehicleContextChange: triggered when the vehicle-selector-form component reports a new valid vehicle selection or when URL parameters for vehicleYear, vehicleMake, or vehicleModel change; calls getMaintenancePlansForVehicle(vehicleYear, vehicleMake, vehicleModel) and renders the returned list.

onPlansLoadSuccess: triggered after a successful getMaintenancePlansForVehicle response; binds each returned item to a result row, including fields such as name, monthly_price, warranty_months, warranty_miles, billing_cycle, and plan_type, as well as the underlying plan object; resets comparison selections for any plans no longer present.
onPlansLoadError: triggered if getMaintenancePlansForVehicle fails; clears the current results list and exposes an error state for display by the UI.
onClickPlanName: triggered when a user clicks on a plan name; reads the corresponding plan_id from the bound data item and navigates to plan_detail.html with planId set to that value.
onClickViewDetails: alternative trigger for viewing plan details; behaves the same as onClickPlanName but may be attached to a separate button or link within the plan row.

onToggleSelectForComparison: triggered when the user toggles the comparison checkbox for a plan; notifies the plan-comparison-toolbar component of the plan_id and its new checked state so that the comparison selection and URL (selectedPlanIds) can be updated.

components.functionality: Maintains the set of plans selected for comparison, synchronizes this set with the URL's selectedPlanIds parameter, and controls the enabled/disabled state of the Compare Selected button. Initiates navigation to plan_compare.html when the user has selected at least two plans and clicks Compare Selected.
event_handlers: onInitFromUrl: called on component initialization; reads the selectedPlanIds query parameter, parses it into an internal set of plan IDs, and updates any comparison checkboxes in the results list that correspond to these IDs.

onSelectionChange: triggered when onToggleSelectForComparison is fired from the maintenance-plan-results component; adds or removes the plan_id from the internal selection set and updates the URL's selectedPlanIds parameter to reflect the new set as a comma-separated string (or removes it if the set is empty). Also recalculates whether the Compare Selected button should be enabled (only when at least two plan IDs are selected).

onVehicleContextChange: triggered when the selected vehicle changes or new plans are loaded; removes from the internal selection set any plan IDs that no longer appear in the current results and updates selectedPlanIds in the URL accordingly.

onClickCompareSelected: triggered when the user clicks the Compare Selected button; if fewer than two plan IDs are in the internal selection set, does nothing (and may expose a validation message). If at least two plan IDs are selected, composes a comma-separated string from the set and navigates to plan_compare.html with planIds set to this string in the query string.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters to keep all components synchronized with the URL. It interprets vehicleYear, vehicleMake, vehicleModel, and selectedPlanIds to initialize component state and updates these parameters when user actions change the underlying state.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: executed when maintenance_plans.html is first loaded; parses the current query string into an internal representation and distributes relevant values (vehicleYear, vehicleMake, vehicleModel, selectedPlanIds) to the vehicle-selector-form and plan-comparison-toolbar components.

onVehicleSelectionUpdate: triggered by the vehicle-selector-form whenever the user selects a new year, make, or model or submits the form; updates URL parameters for vehicleYear, vehicleMake, and vehicleModel and pushes the new state into browser history so that back/forward navigation respects the selection.

onComparisonSelectionUpdate: triggered by the plan-comparison-toolbar whenever the set of selected plan IDs changes; updates the selectedPlanIds URL parameter to a comma-separated list or removes it when the selection is empty.

onUrlChange: triggered when the browser history changes (for example, using back/forward buttons); re-parses the query string and notifies dependent components (vehicle-selector-form, maintenance-plan-results, plan-comparison-toolbar) of updated parameters so that they can re-fetch data or adjust selections as necessary.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom hierarchy as the mockup: a slim yellow utility bar and white header with navigation at the very top, followed by a full-width hero image with the page title "Maintenance Plans" aligned to the left. Directly under the hero, an overlapping main content band appears: on the left, a light, semi-transparent panel contains the vehicle selector form; on the right, the hero/workshop imagery continues to show through, maintaining the two-column feel. Below this overlapping zone, the maintenance plan results and the comparison toolbar form a vertically scrolling content area within the central max-width container, with the toolbar sitting just above the list. The URL state manager has no visual representation and is conceptually tied to the main content area.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom hierarchy as the mockup: a slim yellow utility bar and white header with navigation at the very top, followed by a full-width hero image with the page title "Maintenance Plans" aligned to the left. Directly under the hero, an overlapping main content band appears: on the left, a light, semi-transparent panel contains the vehicle selector form; on the right, the hero/workshop imagery continues to show through, maintaining the two-column feel. Below this overlapping zone, the maintenance plan results and the comparison toolbar form a vertically scrolling content area within the central max-width container, with the toolbar sitting just above the list. The URL state manager has no visual representation and is conceptually tied to the main content area.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The combined yellow utility bar and white header occupy roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport. The hero image beneath them fills about 25–30% of the initial viewport height on desktop, with the "Maintenance Plans" heading anchored in the left-middle. The overlapping vehicle selector panel extends into the lower third of the hero and into the start of the main content area, visually occupying about 50–60% of the content width on larger screens. The remaining right 40–50% in that band is given to the exposed workshop imagery, maintaining the two-column impression. Below this, the comparison toolbar and results list occupy the full width of the central container (approximately 80–90% of the viewport width, up to the site’s max-width, leaving 80–120px margins on each side at large breakpoints). The results list can extend vertically well beyond the first fold, while vertical gaps of roughly 24–32px separate major sections (hero to overlap band, overlap band to results, and between result cards).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays moderate, echoing the brochure-like feel. The vehicle selector form fields are grouped tightly enough to feel efficient but with comfortable breathing room (around 16–24px padding inside the panel and between form rows). The maintenance plan results are presented as individual cards or rows with clear separation (16–24px vertical spacing) and generous line height, ensuring multi-line descriptions remain readable. The comparison toolbar is compact but not cramped, with clear spacing between the status text, selection count, and the compare button. There are no large tables; instead, text, simple icons, and straightforward controls are used, balanced by the large imagery above.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow closely follows the mockup’s F-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left logo, then sweeps across the utility bar and centered navigation. It then drops to the left-aligned "Maintenance Plans" hero title, and from there moves directly down to the left-aligned vehicle selector panel that overlaps the hero. The user’s focus then flows down through the form fields and the primary search/apply button. Once a search is run, attention naturally shifts slightly downward to the comparison toolbar and into the first maintenance plan card in the results list. The right side of the hero and overlapping band is dominated by workshop imagery, acting as a stabilizing secondary visual anchor without competing with the functional left column. As users scroll, the sticky comparison toolbar creates a horizontal anchor at the top of the content area, keeping the flow organized: toolbar at the top, scans down each plan row from left (name/summary) to right (price/compare controls).

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a familiar small-business automotive header: a slim yellow utility strip spanning the full width at the very top with small contact details and icons, immediately followed by a white header containing the workshop logo on the left, a centered horizontal navigation, and a social or contact icon cluster on the right. Beneath this, a full-width hero image of the workshop exterior or signage stretches edge-to-edge; its colors harmonize with the blue-and-yellow brand palette. In the left-middle area of this hero, the page title "Maintenance Plans" appears in bold white type, standing out cleanly against the darker region of the image. As you scroll slightly or simply view the lower half of the hero, a light, semi-transparent white panel appears overlapping the bottom of the hero along the left side, aligned to the central grid. This panel houses the vehicle selector form and occupies roughly the left 60% of the content width, leaving the right 40% to reveal more of the workshop/mechanic imagery, preserving the two-column overlap aesthetic from the original mockup. The panel has soft, flat edges with 16–24px padding and a subtle shadow or contrast so it clearly sits on top of the photo. Below this overlapping band, the background transitions to a solid light backdrop (white or very light grey) within the same central max-width container. Directly under the form, a slim blue-tinted comparison toolbar spans the full width of the content column, holding the selection count and compare action. Under that, the maintenance plan results appear as a vertical stack of cards or rows that extend downward, each separated by comfortable vertical spacing and using a left-heavy layout where plan names and summaries sit on the left while pricing and comparison controls sit on the right. As the user scrolls further down the list, the comparison toolbar remains pinned just under the main header, always available, while the hero and overlapping imagery recede off-screen. Overall, the layout feels stable, trustworthy, and practical, with strong left alignment guiding the eye from hero title to selector form to results, and workshop photography reinforcing the automotive context without cluttering the functional areas.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The vehicle selector form sits in the primary overlapping panel directly beneath the hero, occupying the left side of the main content area in a two-column composition. On desktop, this panel aligns with the central 12-column grid, starting at the left margin of the max-width container and stretching across roughly 7 of 12 columns (about 55–60% of the content width). Vertically, the top edge of the panel overlaps the lower third of the hero image, so about one quarter of the panel’s height is over the photo and the remaining portion extends into the solid content background below. The panel has a light, semi-transparent white background with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides, matching the mockup’s text block style. Inside the panel, a small section title like "Select Your Vehicle" is anchored at the top-left, with a modest 8–12px gap below it before the form fields begin. The Year, Make, and Model controls are arranged horizontally in a row where possible: on large screens each dropdown occupies roughly 1/3 of the panel width with 16px gaps between them; on medium screens they can wrap into two rows while keeping vertical spacing consistent with the 8px base scale. Below the primary dropdown row, an additional row can hold optional fields (e.g., variant, engine) in a similar column structure, or be left empty for a simpler setup. The main action button (e.g., "View Maintenance Plans") is positioned at the bottom-left of the panel, spanning approximately 40–60% of the panel width, with a short supporting link such as "Clear selection" or "Start over" placed to its right or just below in smaller type. Vertical spacing from the last field to the button is around 16–24px, ensuring it feels distinct but connected. The right side of the panel is intentionally kept a bit airier, with slightly more white space between field groups and the right edge to avoid feeling cramped against the workshop image to the right. On tablet and mobile, this panel becomes full-width under the hero, still slightly overlapping the image at the top, but the form fields stack vertically, each taking close to 100% width with 8–16px spacing between them, preserving clarity and readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The form’s containing panel is part of the natural scroll flow but visually overlaps the hero image to create a layered composition. It is not fixed; it scrolls away with the rest of the content. Its width and field arrangement respond to breakpoint changes (two to three columns on desktop, primarily single-column on mobile) while maintaining alignment with the main grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The maintenance plan results section is positioned immediately below the overlapping vehicle selector panel within the same central content column. On desktop, its left edge aligns perfectly with the left edge of the form panel, and its width spans roughly 10 of the 12 grid columns (about 80–85% of the overall page width), keeping generous side margins consistent with the mockup’s content container. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px between the bottom of the form panel and the top of the results section, with the comparison toolbar occupying this space as a cap for the list. The results themselves are presented as a vertical stack of plan cards or rows. Each card spans nearly the full width of the content column, with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px vertical spacing between cards. Inside each card, content follows a left-to-right structure mirroring the F-pattern: on the left two-thirds (around 7–8 of 12 internal columns), the plan name and a short description or key inclusions are arranged in one or two text blocks, using clear hierarchy in typography. On the right third (around 4–5 internal columns), the price, interval information (e.g., "every 10,000 km"), and plan actions (such as a "View details" link and a checkbox or toggle for "Add to compare") are stacked vertically or aligned so that the most important call-to-action sits visually near the middle-right of the card. This right side aligns vertically with the hero’s mechanic imagery above, subtly echoing that right-hand emphasis on action and confirmation. The section can include a simple subheader row at its very top (just below the toolbar) showing something like "X plans found" left-aligned, with sorting or filtering controls aligned to the right, using a slimmer 40–48px tall row to avoid overcrowding. As the user scrolls, the plan cards fill the screen in sequence, and the consistent card width and spacing maintain a neat, brochure-like rhythm. On smaller screens, the results section becomes a full-width column under the hero and form, with each card maintaining the same internal left-to-right logic but allowing elements like price and compare controls to drop beneath the description if space is tight, preserving readability over strict two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The results list is fully scrollable and occupies the main vertical flow of the page beneath the overlapping hero/form area. It is not fixed and will expand as many cards as needed. Within the list, individual cards do not float or stick; the only dynamic behavior influenced by scrolling is from the comparison toolbar above the list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan comparison toolbar sits directly above the first maintenance plan result card, acting as a thin control band that visually separates the vehicle selector area from the result list. It aligns with the same central container as the results, matching their width (about 80–85% of the viewport, centered) and left margin. The toolbar has a modest height of roughly 56–64px, using a subtle blue-tinted background that contrasts gently with the white/neutral page background but is less intense than any primary buttons, so it feels supportive rather than dominant. Inside, content is organized horizontally: on the left, small text such as "Plan comparison" and a secondary line like "Select plans below to compare" or a dynamic count "2 plans selected" stacked on two lines, aligned to the left with 16–24px padding. On the right, aligned to the same vertical center, a primary "Compare" button sits, with a disabled state when no plans are selected and a solid brand-blue state once at least two plans are chosen. If additional controls are needed (e.g., "Clear selection"), they appear as smaller text buttons to the left of the main compare button. Overall, the toolbar content respects the 12-column grid internally, with the left text region spanning about 8 columns and the right button region spanning about 4 columns, giving the button comfortable breathing room near the right edge. The toolbar maintains clear separation from the first result card below with an 8–16px gap, echoing the mockup’s controlled vertical spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The toolbar behaves responsively and uses a sticky positioning pattern within the scroll: when the user is at the top of the results, it appears directly under the vehicle selector panel; as they scroll down through the list, the toolbar sticks to the top of the viewport, just below the main site header, ensuring the compare action is always visible. It spans the full width of the content container and scrolls away only when the user moves above the results section or reaches the very bottom of the page where normal scroll behavior resumes. On smaller screens, its height and padding remain consistent, and the button may center underneath the text when horizontal space is limited, but the sticky behavior remains.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is non-visual and therefore has no direct spatial presence in the layout. Conceptually, it is associated with the main content region that includes the vehicle selector, comparison toolbar, and results list. It listens to interactions in these components and encodes the current vehicle selection, filters, and comparison choices into the URL so that the state can be shared or restored. In terms of mental model, you can imagine it as an invisible layer sitting just behind the central content column, spanning the same vertical region as the selector form and results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The URL state manager occupies no physical space and does not affect layout flow. It behaves purely as a background utility, updating URL parameters and reading them on load or navigation, without pinning, floating, or overlapping any visual component.

name: Maintenance Plan Comparison
title: Maintenance Plan Comparison
description: Compare selected maintenance plans side by side, filter by monthly budget, identify the best warranty within that budget, and navigate to detailed plan information.

core_features: Read the "planIds" URL parameter and call the compareMaintenancePlans interface to load comparison data for the selected maintenance plans.

Display each returned maintenance plan in a side-by-side comparison layout, showing at minimum: plan name, monthly price, warranty duration in months, warranty coverage in miles (if provided), included services summary, and key benefits.
Provide a user-controlled monthly budget input to limit the comparison to plans with a monthly_price less than or equal to the entered value.

Automatically identify and highlight the plan that has the longest warranty_months while remaining at or below the user’s specified maximum monthly price, using warranty_miles as a secondary comparison criterion when applicable.
Allow users to sort the compared plans (e.g., by monthly_price or warranty_months) to change the order in which plans are displayed side by side.
Provide a "Select Plan" button for each compared plan that navigates to plan_detail.html with the relevant planId URL parameter.
Maintain and restore comparison state (selected plan IDs, budget filter, sort order, and highlighted plan) using URL parameters so that the page is deep-linkable and shareable.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Compare plans from the maintenance plans list
1. User selects multiple plans to compare on maintenance_plans.html.
2. maintenance_plans.html navigates to plan_compare.html with a planIds URL parameter containing the selected plan IDs.
3. On page load, plan_compare.html reads planIds and calls compareMaintenancePlans(planIds).
4. The page shows a side-by-side comparison of all returned plans.
5. The user enters a maximum monthly price in the budget filter and submits the filter.
6. The page filters the comparison to plans with monthly_price less than or equal to the user’s input, identifies the plan with the highest warranty_months within that filtered set, and highlights that plan.
7. The user reviews coverage details and optionally changes the sort order (e.g., by price or warranty).
8. The user clicks "Select Plan" under a preferred plan.
9. The browser navigates to plan_detail.html with ?planId set to the chosen planId.

Workflow 2: Shareable comparison with a predefined budget
1. User A is on plan_compare.html, has already loaded some plans via planIds, and has set a budget and sort order.
2. The page encodes planIds, budget, and sort settings into the URL parameters.
3. User A copies the URL and shares it with User B.
4. User B opens the URL; plan_compare.html reads all URL parameters (planIds, budget-related parameter, sort parameter) and calls compareMaintenancePlans(planIds).
5. After data loads, the page applies the budget filter and sort order from the URL and computes/highlights the best plan under the specified budget.
6. User B immediately sees the same comparison context and can click "Select Plan" on any plan to go to plan_detail.html for more details.

Workflow 3: Adjusting comparison criteria on the same page
1. The user lands on plan_compare.html with valid planIds and sees the initial comparison after compareMaintenancePlans returns data.
2. The user changes the sort order using the sort control.
3. The page updates the sort-related URL parameter and re-renders the plan list in the selected order using the already-loaded comparison data (no new API call needed unless planIds change).
4. The user adjusts the budget input multiple times; each submit updates the budget parameter in the URL and recomputes which plan has the best warranty under the new budget.
5. At any point, the user can clear the budget filter via a control, which removes the budget parameter from the URL, restores the full list to view, and removes the budget-based highlight.

Workflow 4: Handling missing or invalid planIds
1. The user opens plan_compare.html without a planIds URL parameter or with a parameter that leads to an empty result from compareMaintenancePlans.
2. The page attempts to call compareMaintenancePlans with the parsed planIds.
3. If the interface returns an empty array or an error indicating invalid/unknown plan IDs, the page shows a functional message indicating that no plans are available for comparison and provides a navigation control back to maintenance_plans.html to select plans.
4. Once the user navigates back and selects valid plans, they are brought again to plan_compare.html with a valid planIds parameter and can proceed with comparison as in other workflows.
interactions:
On page load (initialization):
- Read the planIds URL parameter from the query string.
- If planIds is present and non-empty, call compareMaintenancePlans(planIds) and show a loading state until the data is returned.
- If planIds is missing or empty, show a "no plans selected" state and do not call compareMaintenancePlans.
- After data is loaded, read additional optional URL parameters such as a max monthly budget parameter and a sort parameter, then apply budget filtering, sorting, and highlight computation before rendering the comparison table.

Budget filter interaction:
- The user enters a numeric value into a budget input field representing the maximum acceptable monthly_price.
- On change, the component tracks the current input value in its internal state without yet applying it.
- On submit (e.g., clicking an "Apply" button or pressing Enter), the page updates or adds a dedicated budget-related URL parameter with the entered value.
- The page recomputes the filtered set of plans where monthly_price is less than or equal to the budget value.
- Within this filtered set, the page identifies the plan with the highest warranty_months; if multiple plans share the same warranty_months, it uses warranty_miles (if available) as a secondary comparison; if there is still a tie, it may fall back to the original sort order.
- The identified plan’s ID is stored in page state and optionally reflected in a highlight-related URL parameter so the highlighted plan is preserved on reload or share.

Sort order interaction:
- The user selects a sort option (for example, sort by monthly_price or sort by warranty_months) from a control such as a dropdown.
- The page updates or adds a sort-related URL parameter with the chosen criterion.
- Using the current comparison data already returned from compareMaintenancePlans, the page reorders the plans accordingly before rendering; no additional API call is required.
- Any existing budget filtering remains applied; sorting operates on the filtered list when a budget is active.

"Select Plan" interaction:
- Each plan column includes a "Select Plan" button bound to the plan’s plan_id.
- When the user clicks this button, the page constructs a URL for plan_detail.html including a planId query parameter set to the chosen plan’s ID.
- The page triggers a navigation to plan_detail.html?planId=<chosenPlanId>.
- No additional state from the comparison page is required on the plan detail page; the detail page uses its own logic and interfaces.

Change compared plans (optional control on this page):
- An optional text input can allow advanced users to modify the planIds being compared by entering a comma-separated list of plan IDs.
- On submit, the page updates the planIds URL parameter and reloads the comparison context (either by full page reload or by re-invoking compareMaintenancePlans with the new plan IDs).
- Any budget or sort parameters may be cleared or preserved depending on the implementation; this behavior should be consistent (and reflected by either resetting or maintaining their URL parameters).

Error and empty-state handling:
- If compareMaintenancePlans returns an error, the page shows an error state with appropriate messaging and a retry control that re-triggers compareMaintenancePlans using the current planIds from the URL.
- If compareMaintenancePlans returns an empty array, the page shows a "no plans to compare" state and a navigation control back to maintenance_plans.html.
- These states are fully driven by the API responses and URL parameters, without static data.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is managed entirely via URL parameters and in-memory component state; no localStorage or other persistent browser storage is used.

URL parameters:
- planIds (required for comparison):
  - Format: comma-separated list of maintenance plan IDs.
  - Source: maintenance_plans.html or manual edits.
  - Behavior: On page initialization, the root component parses planIds and passes the value to compareMaintenancePlans(planIds). If this parameter is missing or empty, no API call is made and an informational empty state is shown.

- budgetMax (example name; actual key used consistently across components):
  - Format: numeric value representing the maximum acceptable monthly_price.
  - Behavior on load: If present, the page uses this value to filter the list of plans returned by compareMaintenancePlans and compute the best plan under this budget. The budget input field is pre-populated with this value.
  - Behavior on change: When the user applies a new budget via the budget filter form, the page updates budgetMax in the URL query string (adding it if absent, replacing it if present). The page then recomputes the filtered plan list and the highlighted best-under-budget plan.
  - Behavior on clear: Clearing the budget filter removes budgetMax from the URL and restores the comparison to show all plans; the best-under-budget highlight is removed.

- sortBy (example name; represents current sort criterion):
  - Format: a string representing the active sort criterion (e.g., a small set of known values such as a price-based or warranty-based sort).
  - Behavior on load: If present, the page sorts the loaded comparison data according to the given criterion before rendering.
  - Behavior on change: When the user selects a different sort order, the page updates sortBy in the URL query string and re-sorts the in-memory plan list. The budget filter remains in effect; sorting is applied to the currently visible set of plans.

- highlightPlanId (optional):
  - Format: a single plan ID corresponding to the plan currently highlighted as the best-under-budget recommendation.
  - Behavior on load: If present and the corresponding plan is included in the compareMaintenancePlans response, the page highlights that plan column and/or surfaces it prominently in a helper component.
  - Behavior on recomputation: When the budget filter is applied or changed, the page recomputes the best-under-budget plan and updates highlightPlanId accordingly in the URL. If no plan meets the budget criteria, highlightPlanId is removed.

Interaction with in-memory state:
- In-memory component state is used for transient UI behavior (e.g., the current text in the budget input before submit, loading/error flags, computed, sorted arrays).
- URL parameters are the single source of truth for persisted page state; whenever state changes in a way that should persist across reloads or be shareable (planIds, budgetMax, sortBy, highlightPlanId), corresponding URL parameters are updated.
- On every relevant URL change (e.g., using history.pushState or full navigation), the root component reacts by re-reading the parameters and updating in-memory state. Only changes to planIds cause a new call to compareMaintenancePlans; other parameters operate on already-fetched data.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, data fetching via compareMaintenancePlans, page-level state (loading, errors, filtered/sorted plans, highlighted plan), and passes data and callbacks to child components.
data_binding: compareMaintenancePlans (interface output array of objects including plan: MaintenancePlan and flattened fields)
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered on page load; reads planIds, budgetMax, sortBy, and highlightPlanId from the URL. If planIds is valid, calls compareMaintenancePlans(planIds) and sets loading state.

onDataLoaded: Triggered when compareMaintenancePlans returns successfully; stores the raw plan list, then applies sorting based on sortBy, applies budget filtering based on budgetMax, and computes the highlighted planId if applicable.
onDataError: Triggered if compareMaintenancePlans fails; sets an error state and exposes a retry action.
onBudgetChange: Receives a new budget value from the budget filter component; updates budgetMax in the URL, recomputes the filtered list, and recomputes the highlighted planId.
onSortChange: Receives a new sort criterion from the toolbar; updates sortBy in the URL and re-sorts the current plan list without additional API calls.
onHighlightPlanComputed: When internal logic determines the best-under-budget plan, updates highlightPlanId in the URL if a suitable plan exists, or removes it otherwise.
onSelectPlan: Receives a planId from a child component and triggers navigation to plan_detail.html?planId=<planId>.
onRetryLoad: Called from error/empty-state components to re-call compareMaintenancePlans with the current planIds from the URL.

components.functionality: Abstracts reading and writing of URL query parameters for planIds, budgetMax, sortBy, and highlightPlanId, ensuring consistent encoding/decoding and notifying the root component when relevant state changes.
data_binding: Query string parameters: planIds, budgetMax, sortBy, highlightPlanId
event_handlers: onReadParams: Called by the root component on initialization to obtain the current URL parameter values in a structured form.

onUpdateParams: Called by the root component whenever shareable state (planIds, budgetMax, sortBy, highlightPlanId) changes; updates the browser’s URL query string (using pushState or similar) without reloading unless explicitly directed.
onPopState: Listens for browser navigation events (back/forward) and notifies the root component so it can re-sync its in-memory state with the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Provides controls for showing the number of compared plans, setting a maximum monthly budget, selecting a sort order, and optionally initiating a change to the planIds being compared.
data_binding: compareMaintenancePlans output (for the count of loaded plans)
URL parameter: budgetMax (for initial budget input value)
URL parameter: sortBy (for initial sort selection)
event_handlers: onBudgetInputChange: Updates the local input state as the user types a potential budget value without immediately applying filtering.
onBudgetSubmit: Validates the entered budget, then calls a handler on the root component with the numeric value; the root updates URL and recomputes filtering/highlight.
onBudgetClearClick: Clears the local input, calls a handler on the root component to remove budgetMax from the URL and reset filtering and highlighting.
onSortChange: When the user selects a different sort order, calls a handler on the root component with the selected sort key so the root can update sortBy in the URL and re-sort the plan list.

onChangePlanIdsSubmit (optional): If present, accepts a comma-separated list of plan IDs, and on submit, calls a handler that updates planIds in the URL and triggers a full or logical reload of comparison data.

components.functionality: Displays a summary of the currently highlighted best-under-budget plan (if one exists), helping users quickly identify the plan with the longest warranty within their specified monthly price limit.
data_binding: Filtered plan list derived from compareMaintenancePlans output
event_handlers: onRender: Reads the current filtered list and highlightPlanId; if highlightPlanId is present and matches a plan, displays summary information for that plan (e.g., name, monthly_price, warranty_months, warranty_miles). If budgetMax is set but no plan meets the criteria, displays a message indicating that no plan is available under the current budget.

onChangeBudgetClick (optional shortcut): Provides a shortcut control (e.g., a link or button) that, when clicked, focuses the budget input field in the toolbar or otherwise prompts the user to adjust the budget.

components.functionality: Renders a side-by-side comparison layout where each selected maintenance plan occupies a column and each attribute (e.g., plan name, price, warranty, included services, key benefits) occupies a row.
data_binding: Sorted and optionally filtered plan list derived from compareMaintenancePlans output
event_handlers: onSelectPlanClick: Attached to the "Select Plan" button in each column; passes the corresponding plan_id to the root component’s onSelectPlan handler, which navigates to plan_detail.html with planId set.
onColumnHover (optional): May be used to expose additional hover details, but does not affect persistent state; purely a UI enhancement.
onRowToggleDetails (optional): If included (e.g., for expanding additional details under a plan), toggles local expanded/collapsed state for that plan’s details without modifying URL parameters.

components.functionality: A reusable sub-component representing a single plan within the comparison table, responsible for rendering the plan’s key data and actions.
data_binding: Single item from compareMaintenancePlans output (including plan_id, name, monthly_price, warranty_months, warranty_miles, included_services_summary, key_benefits)
URL parameter: highlightPlanId (to determine whether this column should be visually marked as highlighted)
event_handlers: onSelectPlanClick: Invoked when the plan’s "Select Plan" button is clicked; emits the plan_id to the parent comparison table component.

onFocusFromHelper (optional): If the best-under-budget-helper triggers a request to bring this plan into view, the column listens to a focus event and ensures that its content is visible (e.g., scrolled into view within a horizontally scrollable container).

components.functionality: Displays appropriate messaging and actions when no plans are available for comparison or when an error occurs while loading comparison data.
data_binding: Loading state from the root component
Error state from the root component
event_handlers: onRetryClick: Triggers the root component’s onRetryLoad handler to call compareMaintenancePlans again using the current planIds from the URL.
onGoBackToPlansClick: Navigates the user back to maintenance_plans.html so they can select plans to compare when none are currently available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-stacked pattern as the mockup: global yellow utility bar and main header at the very top (handled by the overall site shell), followed by a full-width hero band with a workshop/signage photo and the page title 'Maintenance Plan Comparison' aligned left. Immediately overlapping the bottom edge of this hero, the main comparison area begins. It uses a two-column layout: the left ~7–8 grid columns contain the interactive comparison tools (toolbar, helper, table), and the right ~4–5 grid columns contain a vertically oriented plan summary column. Components are arranged in a linear top-to-bottom flow within their column, with subtle overlapping of the top content blocks onto the hero image to echo the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-stacked pattern as the mockup: global yellow utility bar and main header at the very top (handled by the overall site shell), followed by a full-width hero band with a workshop/signage photo and the page title 'Maintenance Plan Comparison' aligned left. Immediately overlapping the bottom edge of this hero, the main comparison area begins. It uses a two-column layout: the left ~7–8 grid columns contain the interactive comparison tools (toolbar, helper, table), and the right ~4–5 grid columns contain a vertically oriented plan summary column. Components are arranged in a linear top-to-bottom flow within their column, with subtle overlapping of the top content blocks onto the hero image to echo the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport is used by the existing utility bar and header, and another 25–30% by the hero band. The overlapping comparison section fills the remainder of the first viewport. Within the main content container (max-width centered, with generous horizontal page gutters of ~80–120px), about 60–65% of the width is given to the left comparison column and 35–40% to the right summary column at large breakpoints. Vertical spacing between stacked components within a column is in the 16–32px range, with 32–48px separating the hero and the main comparison block while still maintaining a visually connected overlap.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate and brochure-like. The toolbar and helper banner are compact but given comfortable padding (16–24px) and breathing room around them. The comparison table is allowed to be text-heavy, but rows have clear line height and row spacing to remain readable. The right-hand summary column is more spacious, functioning like a clear, focused panel with a small number of key plan details and possibly one accent image or icon, echoing the mechanic photo’s visual role from the mockup. No element feels cramped; everything uses the 8px spacing scale to keep content tidy and easy to scan.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the F-pattern from the mockup. The eye starts at the top-left logo and navigation, then moves to the hero’s left-aligned 'Maintenance Plan Comparison' title. From there it moves downward into the left comparison column: first to the plan-comparison-toolbar for budget and filter controls, then to the best-under-budget-helper banner, then down through the comparison table. In parallel, the right-hand plan-summary-column acts as a strong secondary anchor, similar to the mechanic photo: after scanning the left content, the eye is drawn rightward to quickly understand the highlighted or best plan. The helper banner and any blue/yellow accents subtly reinforce a Z-like sweep across the top of the comparison area, but reading behavior settles into left-to-right, top-to-bottom.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: Below the site’s standard yellow utility bar and white header with centered navigation, the page introduces a full-width hero section showing a workshop or signage image. On this image, aligned to the left-middle, sits the bold white heading “Maintenance Plan Comparison,” clearly announcing the page purpose. The main comparison content appears to grow out of the bottom of this hero: a light, semi-transparent panel on the left slightly overlaps the hero image, containing the filter toolbar and the ‘best under budget’ helper stacked vertically. To the right, a solid, card-like summary column lines up with the top of this panel, echoing the mechanic-photo column from the mockup. Below the overlapping panel, the comparison table occupies the full height of the left column, scrolling vertically as needed. The right summary column remains visually anchored and slightly elevated, guiding users to the key recommended plan while the left column supports exploration and detailed comparison. The overall effect is structured, trustworthy, and practical: a clean corporate automotive style with clear zones, straightforward typography, and large, functional content blocks.

layout.overall_description: The Maintenance Plan Comparison page sits within the existing site shell: a slim yellow utility bar and white header with logo and navigation span the full width at the very top. Directly beneath them, a full-width hero band uses a workshop or exterior signage photo as a background, with the page title “Maintenance Plan Comparison” set in bold white type toward the left center of the image. The main content container is centered on the page with generous left and right margins, following a 12-column grid. At the bottom edge of the hero, a light, semi-transparent block from the left column slightly overlaps the image, creating a connected transition. This left block contains the comparison toolbar and the ‘best under budget’ helper banner stacked one above the other. To the right, occupying about one-third of the content width, a vertically oriented summary panel begins at the same vertical level, aligning its top with the left block and echoing the right-hand image column in the mockup. Below the overlapping panel on the left, the comparison table extends downward, filling most of the screen’s remaining height and continuing as the page scrolls. The right summary column stays visually prominent and can remain sticky as users scroll the table, ensuring that the best or selected plan is always in view. If there are no plans or an error occurs, a centered message replaces the table area within the left column, preserving the overall layout. The result is a clear, two-column, brochure-like page with moderate content density, strong visual hierarchy, and a reading flow that naturally leads users from the title to the filters, helper guidance, detailed comparison, and finally the highlighted best plan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page root acts as the main content container for this screen, sitting immediately below the global header and hero. It uses a centered, max-width layout (e.g., 1140–1280px) aligned to an implicit 12-column grid, with generous side gutters of approximately 80–120px on large screens and responsive reduction on smaller viewports. Vertically, the root begins just under the hero’s lower edge, with the top of its content overlapping the hero by roughly 24–32px via the top-left content block (toolbar + helper). Inside this root, the layout splits into two main columns: a left content area spanning about 7–8 grid columns (60–65% width) and a right summary column spanning about 4–5 grid columns (35–40% width). All child visual components (toolbar, helper, table, summary column, empty/error state) are anchored within these columns. Vertical spacing between components adheres to the 8px scale, primarily using 16px and 24px paddings within blocks and 24–32px margins between sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable container; position is static within the body, but it coordinates the two-column layout and overlapping effect with the hero.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is a non-visual, logical component that sits at the top of the DOM structure within the page root. It occupies no visible space on the page and does not affect the visual layout directly. Conceptually, it governs the state of filters, selected plans, and budget parameters reflected in the toolbar, helper banner, table, and summary column. Any changes in URL query parameters silently update these visible components, but the state manager itself remains invisible and does not alter spacing or alignment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no rendered footprint. It behaves like an invisible controller that reacts to and updates the layout’s interactive components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison toolbar is positioned at the top-left of the main content area, forming the upper part of the overlapping panel that connects the hero to the comparison section. Horizontally, it sits within the left column and spans roughly 100% of that column (about 7–8 grid columns), while visually appearing as a light card with subtle shadow or border. Its top edge slightly overlaps the bottom edge of the hero image by about 24–32px, echoing the semi-transparent text block overlay from the mockup. Internally, the toolbar arranges its controls (e.g., budget slider, dropdowns, checkboxes) in one or two horizontal rows, aligned left, with approximately 16–24px padding on all sides and 8–16px gaps between controls. There is comfortable whitespace above and below the controls, matching the modest, practical style of the site. The bottom of the toolbar touches or has a small 8–16px gap above the helper banner, creating a visually cohesive stack.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls with the page content. At narrower breakpoints, it may reflow into multiple rows but stays at the top of the main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The best-under-budget-helper sits directly beneath the plan-comparison-toolbar within the left column, forming the lower part of the overlapping left panel. It spans the full width of the left column, matching the toolbar’s width to read as a unified block. The banner has a subtle, attention-catching background—likely a light yellow tint or outlined box—to echo the brand’s yellow utility bar while remaining soft enough not to overpower the table. An icon or small visual cue appears at the left, followed by concise text highlighting the best plan under the current budget; these elements are aligned left and arranged in a single or double line with generous line height. Padding inside the banner is about 16–24px on all sides, and there is a 16–24px vertical gap below it before the start of the comparison table. Visually, when seen together with the toolbar, the helper banner contributes to the sense of a semi-transparent content slab overlapping the hero image, similar in footprint to the 'Logbook Servicing' text block in the mockup, but now dedicated to interactive guidance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls with the page. Its height adjusts based on the amount of helper text, but it maintains consistent padding and vertical spacing relative to the toolbar and table.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan comparison table is anchored directly below the best-under-budget-helper in the left column and occupies the majority of the vertical space in this column. It spans the full width of the left column, aligning its left and right edges with those of the toolbar and helper, creating a clean vertical column of content. The table header row sits about 16–24px beneath the helper banner, with generous top padding to separate the sections. Columns within the table align with the internal grid, presenting plan names, features, and prices in clearly defined columns. Row heights and line spacing are moderate, avoiding tight packing: each row uses at least 40–56px total height to maintain readability. The table can extend below the fold, with users scrolling down the page to see more rows while the right-hand summary column remains visible. At large breakpoints, no horizontal scrolling is required; on smaller screens, the table may become scrollable horizontally while still anchored in this left region. The table forms the visual and functional core of the page, analogous to the long text block under 'Logbook Servicing,' but structured into rows and columns rather than paragraphs.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the left column; it scrolls vertically with the page. On small screens it may introduce horizontal scrolling within its own area while remaining within the main content flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan summary column occupies the right side of the main content area, mirroring the mechanic photo column in the mockup. It starts at approximately the same vertical level as the top of the plan-comparison-toolbar, with its top edge aligned to the top of the overlapping left panel or slightly lower (within 8–16px) to feel visually related. Horizontally, it spans about 4–5 grid columns (35–40% of the content width) and is flush with the right internal edge of the page container. The summary column is designed as a tall, card-like panel with a solid, light background and subtle border or shadow. Inside, content is stacked vertically: at the top, a heading such as “Best Plan for You” or the selected plan’s name; below that, a prominent key figure (monthly cost or warranty length); then a short list of key benefits; and optionally a small illustrative image or brand icon to echo the mockup’s large mechanic photo. Padding is generous (24–32px), and internal spacing uses 8–16px increments. This panel stands out visually as the main reference point for which plan is recommended or currently selected, while still feeling part of the two-column structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: On large screens, the summary column behaves as a sticky sidebar: its container is positioned so that once the user scrolls past the hero, the summary panel remains fixed within the viewport’s right side until the end of the table content. On smaller screens, it collapses below the table and becomes a standard static block to preserve vertical reading flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-or-error-state component appears in place of the main data-driven content when there are no matching plans or an error occurs. It is centered within the left column, occupying the space normally used by the best-under-budget-helper and comparison table. When active, it appears directly beneath the plan-comparison-toolbar with approximately 24–32px of vertical separation, and it spans the full width of the left column. The message area is a simple, light panel or inline block with an icon or symbol at the top, followed by a clear heading and one or two lines of explanatory text—all center-aligned within this left content region. Padding is generous (24–32px) to reduce visual stress and keep the area from feeling empty. The right-hand plan summary column may remain visible but can either show a default prompt (e.g., “Adjust filters to view plans”) or be subtly de-emphasized. The empty/error state preserves the overall two-column structure and page rhythm, using the same margins and grid alignment so the layout does not feel disrupted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the table area within the left column: when the empty/error state is visible, the table and helper content for results are hidden or pushed out of view. It scrolls normally with the page and does not alter the right column’s behavior.

name: Maintenance Plan Detail
title: Maintenance Plan Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected maintenance plan, including included services, intervals, pricing, and enrollment actions, with the ability to add the plan to the cart and navigate to checkout.

core_features: Read the required planId URL parameter and request detailed plan data using getMaintenancePlanDetail.

Display maintenance plan information, including name, description, plan_type, billing_cycle, warranty_months, warranty_miles, and applicable vehicles, based on the MaintenancePlan returned by getMaintenancePlanDetail.
Show pricing details from the plan, including monthly_price and enrollment_fee, and calculate any derived values (such as estimated annual cost) on the client side.
List all included services from the included_services array, showing service_name and each service’s interval_miles and interval_months.
Display the fine_print content returned by getMaintenancePlanDetail with an option to expand/collapse if needed.
Allow the user to enter a preferred maximum monthly budget and compare it against the plan’s monthly_price to understand affordability.
Enroll the user in the plan by calling addMaintenancePlanToCart with the current planId when they click an enrollment action (e.g., "Add Annual Plan to Cart" or "Enroll in Plan").
After a successful addMaintenancePlanToCart call, enable a direct navigation action (e.g., "Go to Cart" or "Proceed to Checkout") that takes the user to cart_checkout.html.
Handle and display loading and error states for both getMaintenancePlanDetail and addMaintenancePlanToCart, including missing or invalid planId.
Prevent duplicate user confusion by tracking in-page state when the plan has been added during the current visit and adjusting button states and messaging accordingly.
user_workflows:
View plan details and consider enrollment:
1. User arrives on plan_detail.html with a planId URL parameter (from maintenance_plans.html or plan_compare.html).
2. The page reads planId and calls getMaintenancePlanDetail(planId).
3. While data is loading, a loading state is shown instead of content.
4. On success, the page shows the plan summary (name, type, description, billing_cycle, warranty), pricing (monthly_price, enrollment_fee), and included services with intervals, along with any fine_print.
5. The user reviews the included services and intervals to confirm they match their maintenance needs.

Check if the plan fits the user’s budget:
1. After the plan details load, the user enters a numeric value in a "maximum monthly budget" input field.
2. When they submit or change the value, the page compares the entered budget to the plan.monthly_price from getMaintenancePlanDetail.
3. The page displays whether the plan’s monthly_price is within or above the user’s entered budget, without altering any data from the APIs.
4. The user can adjust the budget input to explore different affordability thresholds.

Enroll in the maintenance plan and go to checkout:
1. With the plan details visible, the user clicks an enrollment action button such as "Add Annual Plan to Cart" or "Enroll in Plan".
2. The page calls addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId).
3. While the call is pending, the enrollment button is temporarily disabled and a progress indicator is shown.
4. On success, the component receives success, cart, added_item, and message from addMaintenancePlanToCart and updates local page state to mark the plan as added.
5. A success notification is shown using the message from the API, and a "Go to Cart" or "Proceed to Checkout" button becomes enabled.
6. The user clicks "Go to Cart" or "Proceed to Checkout", and the page navigates to cart_checkout.html.
7. The cart_checkout.html page then uses its own logic to display the cart contents, including the newly added maintenance plan.

Handle missing or invalid planId:
1. User lands on plan_detail.html without a planId URL parameter, or with a planId that causes getMaintenancePlanDetail to return an error.
2. The page skips or fails the getMaintenancePlanDetail call and shows an error state indicating that the plan cannot be loaded.
3. Enrollment and pricing sections are hidden or disabled because no plan data is available.
4. The user can modify the URL (outside this page’s scope) to provide a valid planId, or navigate back to maintenance_plans.html or plan_compare.html (handled by site-level navigation, not this page’s APIs).

Handle add-to-cart errors:
1. After viewing plan details, the user clicks the enrollment button.
2. The page calls addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId).
3. If addMaintenancePlanToCart returns success = false or an error occurs, the page shows the message from the response (if available) in an error notification area and keeps the "Go to Cart/Proceed to Checkout" action disabled.
4. The user can retry the enrollment action; each retry triggers a new addMaintenancePlanToCart call.
interactions: On page load: Read the planId query parameter from the URL and, if present, call getMaintenancePlanDetail(planId).
If planId is missing: Skip the API call and show an inline error state; hide or disable any components that require plan data.

On successful getMaintenancePlanDetail response: Bind the returned plan to all plan-related components (summary, pricing, services list, fine print) and render them; clear any previous load error messages.
On getMaintenancePlanDetail failure: Display an error message, log or track the failure in local state, and keep enrollment and pricing interactions disabled.

On user entering or changing the budget input value: Update local component state for the budget value; compare this value against plan.monthly_price and update the affordability message area accordingly.

On user clicking "Add Annual Plan to Cart" or "Enroll in Plan": Validate that plan data and planId are available; call addMaintenancePlanToCart(planId); set a local loading flag to disable the button and show a progress indicator.

On successful addMaintenancePlanToCart response: Store the returned cart and added_item data in local state (for display purposes only), display the message from the API in a success notification area, set a flag that the plan has been added during this session, and enable the "Go to Cart" or "Proceed to Checkout" button.

On addMaintenancePlanToCart failure (success = false or network error): Display the provided message (if any) as an error notification, clear the loading state so the user can retry, and keep the "Go to Cart/Proceed to Checkout" button disabled.

On user clicking "Go to Cart" or "Proceed to Checkout": Navigate the browser to cart_checkout.html without altering the cart via any additional API (the cart contents are already updated by addMaintenancePlanToCart).
On user toggling fine print visibility: Update local boolean state (e.g., isFinePrintExpanded) and show or hide the full fine_print text accordingly; this does not affect API data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state driver is the planId URL parameter. When plan_detail.html loads, the page parses the URL query string and extracts planId. If a non-empty planId is present, the page invokes getMaintenancePlanDetail(planId) exactly once on initial load (or again if the URL changes while the user remains on the page). If planId is absent or invalid, the page enters a 'no-plan' error state where dependent components are disabled. The internal page state (not persisted in localStorage) includes: loading flags for getMaintenancePlanDetail and addMaintenancePlanToCart, the current MaintenancePlan object, the included_services array, the fine_print string, any error or success messages, the user-entered budget value, a derived affordability comparison result (based solely on the budget value and plan.monthly_price), and a boolean indicator that the plan has been successfully added to the cart during the current page session. This 'planAdded' indicator is used to adjust the enrollment button label or disabled state and to enable 'Go to Cart' or 'Proceed to Checkout'. Navigation to cart_checkout.html does not include additional query parameters according to the provided outgoing_connections specification; the checkout page infers the cart from its own logic and the cart object updated by addMaintenancePlanToCart. No persistent storage such as localStorage is used; all state is in memory and driven by the URL and API responses.

components.functionality: On initial load, read the planId from the URL query string and, if present, call getMaintenancePlanDetail(planId). Manage the initial loading and error state for fetching plan details.

Display core information about the maintenance plan, such as plan name, plan_type, billing_cycle, description, warranty_months, warranty_miles, and applicable vehicle information, using the MaintenancePlan object loaded by getMaintenancePlanDetail.

Show detailed pricing for the maintenance plan, including monthly_price and enrollment_fee from the MaintenancePlan, and any client-side derived values (such as estimated annual cost). Provide an input for the user to enter a maximum monthly budget and display whether the plan’s monthly_price is within that budget.

Render the list of included services from the included_services array returned by getMaintenancePlanDetail. For each service, show service_name along with interval_miles and interval_months. Optionally parse the serialized service field for additional details if needed.
Display the fine_print text returned by getMaintenancePlanDetail with a collapsed/expanded view. Users can toggle visibility to read all plan terms and conditions.

Provide enrollment controls for the maintenance plan. When the user clicks the enrollment button (e.g., 'Add Annual Plan to Cart' or 'Enroll in Plan'), call addMaintenancePlanToCart with the current planId. Track the result to show success or error messages, store cart and added_item details for display, and enable a 'Go to Cart' or 'Proceed to Checkout' button that navigates to cart_checkout.html.
Display global status messages related to loading plan details or enrolling in the plan. Shows success messages (e.g., from addMaintenancePlanToCart.message) and error messages for both APIs.

Centralize control of loading flags and high-level error states. Determines when to show the loading indicator, when to render the main plan content, and when to display a 'plan not found' or 'unable to load plan' state based on the presence of planId and the outcomes of getMaintenancePlanDetail.
