layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-stacked, linear flow as the mockup: global utility bar and header (outside this spec), then a full-width hero band controlled by the page-init with a background workshop/building image and a strong left-aligned title. Directly beneath, an overlapping two-column section mirrors the mockup’s service section: on the left, a light text panel containing the plan summary, pricing, and enrollment actions; on the right, a large mechanic/contextual image that aligns to the right edge. Below this hero/overlap block, the content returns to a simple stacked layout: the services list, then a fine-print toggle area. Notification and state-handling components sit near the top and above content respectively, without breaking the main vertical rhythm.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-stacked, linear flow as the mockup: global utility bar and header (outside this spec), then a full-width hero band controlled by the page-init with a background workshop/building image and a strong left-aligned title. Directly beneath, an overlapping two-column section mirrors the mockup’s service section: on the left, a light text panel containing the plan summary, pricing, and enrollment actions; on the right, a large mechanic/contextual image that aligns to the right edge. Below this hero/overlap block, the content returns to a simple stacked layout: the services list, then a fine-print toggle area. Notification and state-handling components sit near the top and above content respectively, without breaking the main vertical rhythm.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 10–15% of the viewport height remains reserved for the site’s utility bar and header (outside this layout), with the hero (page-init) occupying about 25–35% of the initial viewport height. The overlapping plan-detail section below fills the remainder of the first screen: about 55–65% of the viewport height combining the left text panel and right image. The main content container respects generous horizontal margins of around 80–120px on large screens, with all interior paddings and gaps using the 8px base scale (16–24px inner paddings, 32–48px between major sections). The services list and fine print share the same max-width container but are vertically compact enough to feel brochure-like rather than sparse.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is moderate: the plan summary and pricing are grouped in one panel with clear headings and short paragraphs, separated by 16–24px vertical spacing. The services list may be relatively text-heavy, but line height is comfortable and items are grouped by subheadings or bullet lists with consistent spacing. The fine print is initially collapsed to avoid clutter, revealing more dense text only on interaction. The add-to-cart form is concise, limited to essential selections and buttons, with adequate breathing room so it doesn’t feel transactional-heavy. Overall, the page balances narrative explanations with structured lists, similar to a printed brochure.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow intentionally follows an F-pattern: the eye starts at the logo and header (site-wide), then moves to the hero title on the left within the page-init section. Next, it moves down and slightly right into the overlapping left panel where the plan summary heading appears, then continues down into the pricing details and enrollment actions. The large mechanic/workshop photo on the right acts as a strong secondary anchor, visited after the initial text scan. Below the fold, the user naturally continues downward through the services list and fine print. A subtle Z-pattern exists across the top when a notification banner is present, but reading behavior settles into left-to-right, top-to-bottom scanning of text blocks and lists.

layout.overall_description: The Maintenance Plan Detail page sits within the same small-business automotive shell as the mockup: a yellow utility bar and white header/navigation at the very top, spanning the full width. Immediately beneath, the page-init defines a full-width hero section with a wide background photo of the workshop or building signage. A bold, white, left-aligned title such as “Maintenance Plan Details” or the specific plan name is placed in the left-middle of this hero band, occupying roughly the left 40% of the width and about the central third of the hero’s height. This hero occupies around a third of the initial viewport. Just below its lower edge, the main content container (centered with generous side margins) begins an overlapping composition: a light, semi-opaque rectangular panel extends up into the hero by 40–60px and takes roughly the left half of the content width. Within this single panel, the plan summary appears at the top, the pricing details just beneath, and the enrollment actions (add-to-cart form) anchored towards the bottom. On the right, aligned to the same vertical zone, a large right-anchored photo of a mechanic working under a hood fills the remaining half of the width, matching the mockup’s two-column image behavior. Together, these elements fill the remaining height of the first screen. As you scroll down, the overlapping gives way to more conventional stacked sections: the services list spans most of the content width, presented in readable columns or grouped lists, followed by the fine-print toggle section, which initially shows only a concise label and a “View details” control. Notification banners sit just under the header, spanning nearly the full content width but remaining slim in height, while loading and error states temporarily overlay the main content area with a dimmed background and centered messages. The overall impression is practical, clear, and slightly traditional: bold blue and yellow accents, strong photography, and straightforward typography guide the user from plan overview to detailed inclusions and finally to enrollment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-init component controls the main content container and hero-like introduction for this detail page. Directly below the global utility bar and header, it renders a full-width hero band that stretches from the left edge of the viewport to the right. The hero height is approximately 30% of the viewport on desktop, with a minimum height equivalent to about 320–400px. A large workshop or building-signage photo fills this area as a background image, slightly darkened to ensure text contrast. Within this hero, content is aligned to the same centered max-width grid used across the site: the content column is centered with around 80–120px margin from the viewport edges on large screens. Inside that column, the page title (e.g., “Maintenance Plan” and the specific plan name) is left-aligned, positioned around the vertical middle of the hero, occupying roughly 40–50% of the content width. The title uses a bold, white type treatment similar in size and weight to the “Services Provided” heading in the mockup. There is 16–24px of vertical padding around the title block to keep it visually anchored but not cramped. The bottom edge of the hero lightly overlaps the subsequent plan detail section by allowing the next content panel to rise up into its space by about 40–60px, echoing the mockup’s overlapping text block behavior.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the page flow; occupies the full width of the viewport and sets up the overlapping composition with the main content by allowing the next section to visually intrude into its lower area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-banner appears directly beneath the main header/utility bar and just above the hero content, or directly beneath the hero’s heading area when messages relate specifically to this page. It spans the same centered max-width content container as the hero text, typically occupying about 80–90% of the viewport width with the same generous left and right margins. Vertically, it is slim: around 48–64px tall for single-line messages, expanding up to roughly 96px if multiple lines or an icon are present. The banner background uses a solid color from the brand palette (e.g., yellow for general info, red/amber for errors), with left-aligned text and possibly a small status icon on the left. There is 16px padding on all sides of the message, and 16–24px of vertical space separates it from adjacent elements (hero or next content). When active, it subtly shifts the eye into a Z-pattern across the top: logo to notification to navigation, before dropping into the hero title and main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and non-sticky by default; it occupies its place in the vertical flow and pushes other content down when visible. It may be dismissible, collapsing its height smoothly and closing the vertical gap when not present.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-summary-section is placed immediately below the hero, inside an overlapping panel that visually intrudes up into the bottom of the hero by roughly 40–60px. This panel is anchored to the left side of the centered content container, occupying about 55–60% of the content width on desktop (approximately 6–7 out of 12 columns). The panel has a light, semi-opaque white or very light grey background with a subtle shadow or border to distinguish it from the hero image behind it, and uses 24px padding on all sides. Within this panel, the plan summary appears at the top: a bold heading showing the plan name (left-aligned, roughly the same size as a section heading in the mockup) followed by 1–3 short paragraphs or bullet points summarizing the plan’s purpose, intervals, and vehicle applicability. Each paragraph has around 8–12px spacing between them. The top of this panel aligns roughly with the lower third of the hero image, reinforcing the layered effect. The bottom of the panel continues downward well beyond the hero’s bottom edge, providing enough space to also include the pricing and enrollment components beneath the summary in a single cohesive block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a key content card within the flow: it overlaps visually with the hero above but is otherwise static. On narrower screens, it expands toward 100% width and the overlapping effect is reduced so that the panel sits directly beneath the hero with standard spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-pricing-section lives directly beneath the summary content inside the same left-hand overlapping panel. It does not create a separate card; instead, it reads as the second block within the unified panel. After a 16–24px vertical gap below the last summary paragraph, the pricing heading (“Pricing & Billing”, for example) appears left-aligned. Below it, key price points—such as monthly cost, total term cost, and any setup fees—are displayed in a clear hierarchy: the main price is larger and may be bold or colored in the brand’s blue, while supporting details are in regular body text. The pricing module typically uses 2–3 rows of information, possibly in a simple 2-column grid (label on the left, value on the right) that spans approximately 70–80% of the panel width, leaving modest whitespace on the right. Vertical spacing between each pricing row is around 8–16px. This section occupies roughly the middle third of the overlapping panel’s height, keeping it visually between the descriptive summary above and the enrollment actions below. It feels integrated into the reading flow: eye moves from summary heading to pricing details without a strong visual break.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the overlapping left panel; it does not introduce new borders or backgrounds. Its layout adjusts responsively, collapsing any 2-column label/value alignment into a single column stack on smaller screens while preserving vertical spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-enrollment-actions component is positioned at the bottom of the same overlapping left panel, directly beneath the pricing section with about 24px of vertical separation. It occupies the full width of the panel’s interior (roughly 55–60% of the content area on desktop) but keeps its controls visually compact. At the top of this sub-area, a short label or prompt (e.g., “Ready to enroll?”) appears in medium-weight text. Below that, any necessary inputs (such as vehicle selection from a dropdown or start date) are laid out horizontally where possible—e.g., two small controls side-by-side each taking about 45% of the panel width—with 16px separation between them, or stacked vertically with 8–12px gaps if space is tight. The primary call-to-action button (e.g., “Add Plan to Cart”) is left-aligned beneath the inputs, stretching to about 50–60% of the panel width and using the brand’s primary color (blue) with adequate padding (12–16px vertically). A smaller secondary text link or button (“View Cart” or “Need help?”) can sit to the right of or just below the primary button with 8–12px spacing. The bottom padding of the panel (24px) ensures that the enrollment controls don’t feel cramped against the panel’s lower edge. Visually, this area anchors the overlapping block and concludes the first-screen narrative by funneling attention toward the main action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the overlapping panel; it scrolls out of view with the page. On smaller screens, controls stack vertically and buttons stretch closer to full width, but they remain in the same relative position (beneath pricing, within the same left-hand card).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-services-list section begins immediately below the overlapping hero/panel zone, once the left panel and right-side mechanic image end. It is part of the main centered content column and spans approximately 80–90% of the content width, with the same left and right margins as the hero text. There is a generous 32–40px vertical gap between the bottom of the overlapping panel and the top of this section, visually signaling a new content block. At the top, a clear, left-aligned heading such as “What’s Included” introduces the section. Below, the services are presented in a structured but readable format: on larger screens, two columns of lists each taking about 48% of the width with 4–8% gutter between them, aligning to the implicit 12-column grid. Each service item uses a short label (possibly bold) followed by a brief description on the same line or directly below, with 8–12px vertical spacing between items and 16–24px spacing between groups or subheadings. The density is brochure-like: enough items to fill a substantial vertical area (roughly 1–1.5 viewport heights as needed) but with sufficient line height and spacing so the eye can scan down in comfortable blocks. The section background remains plain white, relying on headings and spacing for separation rather than heavy card styling.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling section within the main flow. On smaller screens, the two-column layout collapses to a single column, with items stacking vertically while preserving the same vertical spacing and heading hierarchy.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The fine-print-section is placed directly beneath the services list, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space. It spans the same width as the services list within the centered content container but is visually lighter and more compact. At the top, a left-aligned label such as “Terms & Conditions” or “The Fine Print” appears, accompanied by a small chevron icon or ‘+’ indicator on the right, signaling its expandable nature. This label row is contained in a subtle, low-contrast bordered bar or lightly shaded strip that runs across about 100% of the section width, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16px horizontal padding. When collapsed, only this bar is visible, keeping the page relatively clean and in line with the mockup’s avoidance of clutter. When expanded, the fine print text appears directly below this bar, indented to the same 16px padding and spanning the width of the section. The fine print can be more text dense, with smaller font size and single-column layout, but lines are still set with comfortable line height and 8–12px spacing between paragraphs. The entire fine-print block, when expanded, might occupy 1/3 to 1/2 of a viewport height, depending on length.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline and collapsible; it expands and collapses without affecting the fixed position of any other components, simply pushing content below down or pulling it up as needed. It does not stick or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-and-error-state component overlays or replaces the main content area depending on status. During initial page load, a semi-transparent white or light-grey overlay can cover the entire viewport area below the header, including the hero and overlapping panel, slightly dimming the background while a centered spinner or progress indicator appears in the middle of the screen. This central indicator is positioned horizontally at the center of the viewport and vertically around the center or slightly above center, with a short status message beneath it. The overlay respects the site’s grid visually but floats above all other components, making it clear that data is being fetched. For error states (e.g., failure to load plan details), a prominent message card is centered within the content column, roughly 50–60% of the content width, with 24px padding and a subtle border, placed where the overlapping panel would normally appear. The message is left-aligned within this card, with a brief description and possibly a retry button aligned to the left below the text. This ensures the error is immediately noticeable without feeling visually jarring.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The loading overlay is temporarily fixed over the main content region (beneath the global header) with a high z-index, preventing interaction with underlying components until loading completes. The error state card replaces the usual hero-overlap content in the normal flow, pushing other sections (services list, fine print) down or, if data is unavailable, hiding them entirely and occupying their place.

name: Request a Quote
title: Request a Quote
description: Page for requesting a service quote by selecting a quote-eligible service, nearby location, preferred visit time, and providing contact details.

core_features: Load available quote-only services into a service selector using getQuoteServiceOptions.
Allow users to enter a ZIP code and retrieve nearby locations and time slots for the selected service using getQuoteAvailabilityByZip.
Display a date/time selector based on returned time slots, automatically highlighting the earliest available morning time within the next 3 days in a configurable morning time window.
Allow selection of visit type (drop_off, wait, shuttle) with shuttle availability constrained by the chosen location’s data.
Collect contact information (name, phone, email) and a free-text issue description.
Submit the quote request using submitQuoteRequest and display the returned confirmation message without leaving the page.
user_workflows: Primary quote request workflow: (1) User lands on quote_request.html, optionally with URL parameters for serviceId and zip. (2) Page loads quote-only services via getQuoteServiceOptions and preselects the service indicated in the URL (if present and valid). (3) User enters or confirms their ZIP. (4) When both a service and ZIP are available, page calls getQuoteAvailabilityByZip with daysAhead set to 3 and a configured morning time window. (5) Page displays nearby locations and their available time slots, highlighting the earliest available morning slot within the 3-day range. (6) User selects a location and preferred time slot. (7) User selects visit type (drop_off, wait, or shuttle, with shuttle enabled only if the location supports it). (8) User fills in contact info and issue description. (9) User clicks the Submit Quote Request button, which triggers submitQuoteRequest with all collected data. (10) Page displays the returned confirmation_message and a summary of the submitted quote details.

Deep-link workflow from service listing: (1) User clicks a link from another page such as quote_request.html?serviceId={id}. (2) Page preselects the service corresponding to serviceId once getQuoteServiceOptions completes. (3) User enters ZIP, then availability is loaded via getQuoteAvailabilityByZip. (4) User completes the remaining steps (location/time, visit type, contact info) and submits as in the primary workflow.

Deep-link workflow with prefilled service and ZIP: (1) User navigates to quote_request.html?serviceId={id}&zip={zip}. (2) After services are loaded and serviceId is resolved, page automatically calls getQuoteAvailabilityByZip using the serviceId and zip from the URL. (3) Earliest morning slot in the next 3 days is highlighted. (4) User confirms or changes the suggested location/time, selects visit type, adds contact and issue details, and submits via submitQuoteRequest.

Edit selection workflow: (1) After choosing location, time, and visit type, user can change any of these inputs (service, ZIP, location, time, or visit type). (2) URL parameters are updated accordingly. (3) If service or ZIP changes, the page re-calls getQuoteAvailabilityByZip to refresh options. (4) Summary and eventual submission use only the latest selections reflected in the URL and form state.
interactions: Service selection interaction: User changes the service dropdown; page updates the serviceId URL parameter, clears any stale availability-related selections (locationId, requestedDatetime, timeSlotId) from the URL, and if a ZIP is already present, immediately re-calls getQuoteAvailabilityByZip.

ZIP entry interaction: User enters a ZIP and submits (e.g., presses Enter or clicks a Find Availability button); page updates the zip URL parameter, validates that a service is selected, and then calls getQuoteAvailabilityByZip with the current serviceId, zip, daysAhead=3, and the configured morning time window.

Availability selection interaction: After getQuoteAvailabilityByZip returns, user selects a location and time slot from the displayed options; page updates URL parameters (locationId, requestedDatetime and/or timeSlotId) and updates the summary section. The earliest available morning time slot within the 3-day range is automatically highlighted when options are first shown.

Visit type selection interaction: User selects visit type via radio buttons or a dropdown. The component enables or disables 'shuttle' based on the offers_free_shuttle property of the selected location from the availability results. Selection updates the visitType URL parameter.

Contact info entry interaction: User fills in name, phone, email, and issue_description in a form. Client-side validation ensures required fields are present and email/phone formats are acceptable before allowing submission.

Quote submission interaction: User clicks the Submit Quote Request button. Page validates that serviceId, locationId, requestedDatetime, visitType, name, phone, and email are all present and valid. On success, it calls submitQuoteRequest with the current values. While the request is pending, the button is disabled and a loading state is shown. On success, the confirmation_message and QuoteRequest data are displayed; on error, an error message is shown.

URL-driven state interaction: When the URL changes (e.g., via navigation or internal updates with pushState/replaceState), the state manager component re-reads parameters (serviceId, zip, locationId, requestedDatetime, timeSlotId, visitType) and rehydrates the appropriate selections in the service selector, ZIP field, availability picker, and visit type selector, triggering new API calls when necessary (such as when serviceId or zip changes).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters to control and restore state. Supported parameters include: serviceId (ID of the selected Service for the quote), zip (user-entered ZIP for location search), locationId (ID of the selected Location from availability results), timeSlotId (ID of the selected AppointmentTimeSlot when available), requestedDatetime (preferred datetime for the quote, derived from the selected time slot), and visitType (one of drop_off, wait, shuttle). On initial load, the state manager reads these parameters: if serviceId is present, it preselects that service after getQuoteServiceOptions returns. If both serviceId and zip are present, it calls getQuoteAvailabilityByZip with daysAhead set to 3 and a configurable morning time window, then selects and highlights the earliest available morning time slot within the returned time_slots across locations. If locationId and timeSlotId or requestedDatetime are present and match returned data, they are preselected in the availability picker and reflected in the summary. Any change to service selection or ZIP input results in an update to the URL parameters and a fresh call to getQuoteAvailabilityByZip; previously selected locationId/timeSlotId/requestedDatetime are cleared when they are no longer valid. Visit type changes update the visitType parameter but do not trigger API calls. The confirmation state is not persisted in URL parameters; it is shown only immediately after a successful submitQuoteRequest call during the current page session.

components.functionality: Central controller that reads and writes URL parameters, orchestrates when to call getQuoteServiceOptions and getQuoteAvailabilityByZip, and distributes current selections (service, zip, availability, visit type) to other components.

Loads quote-only services using getQuoteServiceOptions and renders them in a dropdown. Allows the user to select a service for which they want a quote. When a service is selected, updates the URL (serviceId) and notifies the state manager to potentially refresh availability.

Captures the user’s ZIP code and, when combined with a selected service, initiates an availability search via getQuoteAvailabilityByZip with daysAhead=3 and a configured morning time window. It synchronizes the ZIP input with the zip URL parameter and triggers availability refreshes whenever the ZIP or service changes.

Displays locations and their available time slots returned from getQuoteAvailabilityByZip. Identifies and visually highlights the earliest available morning time slot within the next 3 days based on the configured morning time window and the is_available flag of AppointmentTimeSlot entries. Allows the user to select a location and time slot, then updates URL parameters (locationId, timeSlotId and requestedDatetime) and informs the state manager.

Provides controls for choosing visit type (drop_off, wait, shuttle). Uses the currently selected location’s data (offers_free_shuttle) to enable or disable the shuttle option dynamically. Updates the visitType URL parameter and exposes the selected value to the main quote submission workflow.

Collects required contact fields (name, phone, email) and an optional free-text issue_description from the user. Validates that required fields are not empty and that phone/email formats are acceptable. On successful validation, passes the data to the parent controller when the Submit Quote Request button is clicked.

Displays a consolidated summary of the current selections: chosen service (name and category), selected location (using Location details from availability results), selected requestedDatetime/time slot, and current visit type. Updates automatically whenever any of these inputs change. Provides optional edit triggers (e.g., links or buttons) that focus the relevant components by updating URL parameters or scrolling.

Coordinates the final submission of the quote request. Gathers the selected serviceId, locationId, visitType, requestedDatetime (derived from the selected AppointmentTimeSlot or equivalent selection), and the contact and issue details from contact-info-form. Performs final validation and calls submitQuoteRequest. Handles loading, success, and error states and triggers display of the confirmation component on success.

Displays the confirmation_message and key details from the returned QuoteRequest after a successful submitQuoteRequest call. This component appears inline on the page without navigation away. It may include an action to start a new quote, which clears relevant URL parameters and local form state while staying on quote_request.html.

Shows loading and error states for calls to getQuoteServiceOptions, getQuoteAvailabilityByZip, and submitQuoteRequest. Aggregates status information from these operations so the user is informed of ongoing requests or failures.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: slim yellow utility bar, white header/navigation, then a full-width hero image with a left-aligned title 'Request a Quote'. Directly beneath the hero, a main quote section uses an overlapping, two-column composition. The left column is a light, semi-transparent panel that contains the quote request steps (service, location, time, visit type, contact details, submit). The right column hosts the quote summary and a tall workshop/mechanic image, mirroring the text-plus-photo arrangement from the mockup.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same linear, top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: slim yellow utility bar, white header/navigation, then a full-width hero image with a left-aligned title 'Request a Quote'. Directly beneath the hero, a main quote section uses an overlapping, two-column composition. The left column is a light, semi-transparent panel that contains the quote request steps (service, location, time, visit type, contact details, submit). The right column hosts the quote summary and a tall workshop/mechanic image, mirroring the text-plus-photo arrangement from the mockup.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and utility bar together occupy about the top 10–15% of the viewport. The hero section takes up roughly 25–30% of the viewport height, with the quote title in the left-middle. The overlapping quote content block begins around the lower third of the hero and extends downward, filling the remainder of the first viewport. Within this block, the left form column spans about 7 of 12 grid columns (~60–65% width), while the right summary/image column spans about 5 of 12 columns (~35–40% width). All blocks use horizontal padding of 16–24px and vertical spacing between sections of around 24–32px, aligned to the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Forms are broken into clear sections with comfortable spacing between field groups, avoiding overly long, uninterrupted forms. Each component uses modest padding (16–24px) and generous line height, matching the brochure-like density of the mockup. The page balances text-heavy form content on the left with a large photo and compact summary on the right so the screen never feels cluttered, even though there are multiple steps.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern: the user looks left to the logo, scans across the header and navigation, then drops down to the left-aligned hero title 'Request a Quote'. From there, the eye moves straight down into the left-side form steps, starting with the service selector at the top. After understanding the main form, attention is pulled rightward to the quote summary and supporting photo. The primary CTA (submit) sits near the bottom-left of the main content block, completing a natural top-left to bottom-left flow, with occasional glances to the right column for confirmation of chosen options.

layout.overall_description: The page sits inside the familiar site frame: a full-width yellow utility bar at the very top, a centered white header with logo, navigation, and social icon, and then a broad hero image of the workshop or building signage stretching edge to edge. Over this hero, on the left-center, a bold white headline reads 'Request a Quote', anchoring the page purpose. As you scroll just slightly, a large, light-colored panel slides up from the bottom edge of the hero and slightly overlaps it, forming the main quote module. This panel occupies the left two-thirds of the content area and is organized into stacked, clearly labeled sections for selecting a service, entering a location, picking a time, choosing visit type, and entering contact details, with the submit action at the bottom. To the right, taking the remaining third of the width, a tall photo of a mechanic at work runs from near the top of the overlapping area downwards, with a compact summary card in front of or just below it. The overall effect closely mirrors the original mockup’s services page: structured, trustworthy, and practical, with a strong left-hand content focus and a reinforcing visual on the right, all framed by generous side margins and consistent vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component does not render any visible UI. It conceptually wraps the entire quote area, spanning the full width of the main content container beneath the hero image and encompassing both the left form column and the right summary/image column. It lives at the top of the document flow for the quote section, acting as an invisible layer that manages state for all visible quote components within the overlapping content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logical wrapper that follows normal document flow but occupies no visible space; all other quote components are nested inside its boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service selector sits at the top of the left-hand quote panel, directly below the lower edge of the hero overlap, making it the first interactive element users encounter. It spans nearly the full width of the left column (about 7 of 12 grid columns), with 16–24px of internal padding and a clear section heading like 'Choose a Service'. There is 24–32px of whitespace above its content area separating it from the hero boundary, and about 24px below it before the next form section. On larger screens, it aligns flush to the left margin of the main content container, with at least 80–120px of space from the browser edge, mirroring the text block alignment in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main left column, always occupying the topmost section of the quote form stack and pushing subsequent components downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The location/ZIP form appears immediately beneath the service selector within the same left-side panel, maintaining the vertical stacking order. It spans the same width as the service selector, creating a continuous left alignment edge. Internally, fields may be arranged in one or two columns (e.g., ZIP and suburb side by side) but remain visually balanced and comfortably spaced. There is a consistent vertical gap of 24px from the bottom of the service selector section heading to the top of this block, giving it clear separation while still feeling part of a single flow within the left column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section stacked in the left column, following the service selector; it expands vertically as needed based on field count but does not affect the alignment of the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The availability picker is positioned directly below the location/ZIP form, still within the same overlapping left panel. It stretches the full width of the left column and introduces a slightly more spacious vertical area (e.g., a grid of preferred dates and time slots) while respecting the 16–24px internal padding. The top of this section sits about 24–32px beneath the location form, and its heading aligns left with all previous section titles, supporting a clean, ladder-like left edge. Visually, this section occupies a noticeable but not overwhelming vertical portion, roughly 15–20% of the left panel’s height in the first viewport, making it a key middle step in the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the left column that may increase the overall height of the quote area; it scrolls normally with the page, preserving the overlapping relationship to the hero only at the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The visit-type selector sits beneath the availability picker, occupying the same left-column width and aligning with the established left margin. It is given a compact vertical footprint — perhaps a row of radio buttons or pill-style options — with enough padding to keep it readable (about 16px top and bottom). The spacing above and below remains consistent with other sections (around 24px), so it feels like a distinct yet lightweight step in the sequence before contact details. When viewed in the initial fold, it often appears toward the lower-middle of the left panel, just before the user scrolls further down.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked section within the left column, adjusting height minimally and maintaining alignment with surrounding left-side form elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info form occupies the lower portion of the left quote panel, directly below the visit-type selector and above the submit controls. It spans the full width of the left column, with fields arranged in one or two columns depending on screen width (e.g., name fields side by side, email and phone stacked), but always staying within the 7-column grid allocation. It uses more vertical space than individual selectors, potentially filling a significant part of the lower half of the first or second viewport, but is broken into clearly labeled rows with 8–16px spacing between fields to maintain the moderate density of the design. There is a consistent margin of 24–32px below this section before the submit controller, visually signaling the end of data entry.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the left column; it grows vertically as needed and pushes the submit controls downward while keeping the overall left-right column relationship intact.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quote summary sits in the right column, aligned to the top of the overlapping area so that its top edge roughly lines up horizontally with the top of the service selector on the left. It occupies about 4–5 of the 12 grid columns (≈35–40% width), centered within the right content area and inset with 16–24px padding. Visually, it is either layered as a card over the lower portion of the mechanic/workshop photo or placed directly below a tall photo header, creating a clear visual tie between the summary and the imagery. Vertically, it takes up about 25–35% of the right column’s height in the first viewport, leaving space above or behind for the photo while providing a succinct, easily scannable recap of selected options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; it scrolls in sync with the left form content and maintains its relative top alignment with the left column’s first elements at larger breakpoints. On narrow screens it would stack below the form, but still follow the same padding and spacing rules.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submit controller (containing the primary 'Request Quote' button and any supporting text or consent checkbox) is anchored at the bottom of the left column’s main content block, directly beneath the contact-info form. It spans the full left-column width, but the primary button itself is left-aligned, echoing the mockup’s focus on the left side of the layout. There is a generous vertical padding of 16–24px around this controller and a slightly larger top margin (around 32px) above it to visually separate it from the last field, making the CTA feel distinct. Below the submit area, a modest buffer of 32–48px separates it from the bottom of the overlapping panel, ensuring the page does not feel cramped at the fold.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static at the bottom of the left column’s content stack; it moves down as the user scrolls and does not remain fixed or sticky, maintaining the straightforward, linear interaction pattern.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Upon successful submission, the confirmation message appears as a full-width feedback banner within the main content container, directly above the left quote panel and spanning across both the left and right columns. It is positioned just below the lower boundary of the hero (or where the overlapping panel begins), pushing the quote panel slightly downward while remaining clearly associated with it. The banner height is modest (roughly 56–80px), with centered text and 16–24px of vertical padding, visually echoing the slim but prominent yellow utility bar at the very top of the page. This placement ensures users see the feedback immediately without losing the context of the form they just submitted.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal flow between the hero and the overlapping quote section; it may slide in or fade in but otherwise remains static and scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API status indicator is a small, subtle element positioned inside the left quote panel, near the top-right corner of the panel’s inner content area, horizontally aligned with the service selector’s heading. It occupies minimal space (a small icon and short label), sitting inline with or just above the first section heading, with about 8–12px of spacing from the top edge and right padding of 16–24px. Its placement makes it easy to notice when loading or error states occur, but it remains visually subordinate to the main form labels and buttons, avoiding distraction from the primary flow of completing the quote request.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, anchored to the top-right of the quote panel content area; it does not scroll independently or float, and its presence does not alter the positioning of other form components beyond a small reserved corner.

name: Gift Cards
title: Gift Cards
description: Configure and purchase a digital or physical gift card for automotive services, then add it to the cart and proceed to checkout.

core_features: Load available gift card products and their delivery methods using getGiftCardProducts.
Allow the user to choose a delivery method by selecting from available gift card products (e.g., an email gift card product).
When a product is selected, load its detailed configuration (preset amounts and custom amount limits) using getGiftCardProductDetail.

Provide selection of a gift card amount from the product’s preset_amounts and/or a validated custom amount within min_custom_amount and max_custom_amount. Any preset equal to 100 that is returned by the API is presented as a selectable option.
Collect required recipient and sender information (names and email addresses) plus an optional personal message.
Validate the configured gift card (product, amount, recipient/sender details) before submission.
Call addGiftCardToCart to create a GiftCard instance, add it to the Cart, and receive updated Cart and CartItem data.
After a successful add-to-cart, display a confirmation state and a "Proceed to Checkout" action that navigates to cart_checkout.html with relevant URL parameters (such as cartId) when available.
user_workflows:
Configure and purchase an email gift card:
1) User visits gift_cards.html (optionally with URL parameters such as productId or deliveryMethod).
2) On page load, getGiftCardProducts is called to retrieve all active GiftCardProduct entries.
3) The page determines the initially selected product based on URL parameters (productId and/or deliveryMethod), or defaults to the first active product if no valid parameter is provided.
4) When a product is selected, getGiftCardProductDetail is called with the chosen giftCardProductId.
5) The user selects an amount from the product’s preset_amounts or enters a custom amount within the allowed min_custom_amount and max_custom_amount.
6) The user enters recipient_name, recipient_email, sender_name, sender_email, and optionally a personal_message.
7) The user submits the form via the "Add Gift Card to Cart" button, triggering validation.
8) If validation passes, addGiftCardToCart is called with the selected productId, amount, deliveryMethod (taken from the selected product), recipient and sender details, and personal_message.
9) If addGiftCardToCart returns success, the page displays a confirmation state and enables the "Proceed to Checkout" button.
10) The user clicks "Proceed to Checkout" and is navigated to cart_checkout.html, optionally including cartId from the addGiftCardToCart response as a URL parameter.

Switching between delivery methods (e.g., email vs physical mail) before purchase:
1) User arrives on gift_cards.html; getGiftCardProducts loads all products.
2) Delivery methods available on the page are derived from the delivery_method field of the returned GiftCardProduct list.
3) The user clicks a delivery method option; the page selects a GiftCardProduct whose delivery_method matches that option.
4) getGiftCardProductDetail is called for the newly selected product, and amount options are updated accordingly.
5) Previously entered recipient/sender details are preserved in page state unless the user leaves or reloads the page.
6) The user proceeds as in the main workflow to select an amount, fill in details, and call addGiftCardToCart.

Preselecting a product and amount via URL parameters (e.g., for a preset promotion):
1) A user follows a link to gift_cards.html that includes query parameters such as productId and/or amount.
2) On page load, getGiftCardProducts is called.
3) If productId is present and corresponds to an active GiftCardProduct, that product is preselected; otherwise the first active product is used.
4) getGiftCardProductDetail is called for the selected product.
5) If an amount URL parameter is present, the page attempts to preselect it as follows:
   - If it matches one of the product’s preset_amounts, that preset is selected.
   - Otherwise, if it is numeric and within the product’s min_custom_amount and max_custom_amount, it is filled into the custom amount field.
   - If it is invalid, the page ignores the amount parameter and leaves the amount unselected.
6) The user can then adjust details and complete the purchase normally.
interactions: On page load, the page triggers getGiftCardProducts (no parameters) to fetch all available gift card products.

When the URL parameters change (for example via internal navigation that updates productId, deliveryMethod, or amount), the page recalculates which GiftCardProduct should be selected and, if necessary, calls getGiftCardProductDetail for the new product.

When the user selects a delivery method option, the page updates the selected GiftCardProduct to one whose delivery_method matches and updates the URL parameter deliveryMethod (and productId for that product).

When the user selects a specific gift card product (if multiple are shown explicitly), the page updates the selected productId in state and in the URL, and calls getGiftCardProductDetail with that giftCardProductId.
When getGiftCardProductDetail returns, the page populates the amount selection component with the returned preset_amounts and custom amount bounds.
When the user clicks a preset amount button, that amount is stored as the selected amount, any custom amount input is cleared, and the amount is reflected in the summary panel.

When the user types in the custom amount field, the preset selection is cleared, the entered value is validated against min_custom_amount and max_custom_amount (range and numeric format), and any validation errors are displayed.

When the user types into the recipient_name, recipient_email, sender_name, sender_email, or personal_message fields, the page updates the in-memory GiftCard configuration state and clears field-level validation errors as appropriate.

When the user clicks the "Add Gift Card to Cart" button, the form submission handler validates that: a product is selected, a valid amount is chosen, required names and emails are present and in a valid format. If validation fails, the submission is blocked and errors are shown.

If validation passes, the submission handler calls addGiftCardToCart with the selected giftCardProductId, chosen amount, the selected product’s delivery_method, and the recipient/sender data and personal_message. The button is temporarily disabled, and a loading state is indicated.
If addGiftCardToCart returns success=false, the page displays the message property from the response in an error notification area and re-enables the "Add Gift Card to Cart" button.

If addGiftCardToCart returns success=true, the page stores the returned Cart, GiftCard, and CartItem in memory, shows a success/confirmation message, updates URL parameters to reflect the successful state if desired (for example, addedGiftCardId and cartId), and displays an enabled "Proceed to Checkout" button.
When the user clicks "Proceed to Checkout" after a successful add, the page navigates to cart_checkout.html, optionally appending cartId from the addGiftCardToCart response as a URL parameter.

If the user changes the selected product or delivery method after a successful add, the confirmation state can remain visible for the last added item, but subsequent submissions will add additional gift cards via new calls to addGiftCardToCart.
page_functionality.state_logic:
Page state is coordinated with URL parameters to support deep linking and back/forward navigation without using localStorage.

URL parameters used:
- productId: The ID of the GiftCardProduct to select on load.
- deliveryMethod: A delivery method value corresponding to GiftCardProduct.delivery_method (e.g., 'email' or 'physical_mail').
- amount: A numeric value representing a desired preselected amount.
- addedGiftCardId (optional): An identifier of a GiftCard that was just added, used to show a confirmation state after redirects if desired.
- cartId (optional): The current Cart.id returned by addGiftCardToCart, used when navigating to cart_checkout.html.

Resolution rules on page load:
1) After getGiftCardProducts returns, the page determines the initial selected product:
   - If productId is present and matches an active GiftCardProduct, that product is selected.
   - Else if deliveryMethod is present, the page selects the first GiftCardProduct whose delivery_method matches deliveryMethod.
   - Else, the page selects the first active GiftCardProduct in the returned list.
   - If no products are returned, the page shows an informational state that no gift card products are currently available and disables the configuration form.
2) Once a product is selected, the page calls getGiftCardProductDetail with its ID and stores the detailed configuration.
3) After product detail is loaded, the page interprets the amount parameter:
   - If amount is present and parseable as a number, and it matches one of the product’s preset_amounts, that preset is selected.
   - Else if amount is present, numeric, and within the inclusive range [min_custom_amount, max_custom_amount], it is placed in the custom amount field and treated as the currently selected amount.
   - Otherwise, the amount parameter is ignored and the amount remains unselected until the user chooses one.
4) If addedGiftCardId is present in the URL, the page can show a generic confirmation banner indicating that a gift card was successfully added; the specific details displayed should rely on in-memory state from the latest addGiftCardToCart call if available.
5) Whenever the user changes the selected product or delivery method, the page updates the productId and deliveryMethod URL parameters using pushState/replaceState, without a full reload.
6) When the amount is changed by user interaction (preset button or custom input), the page updates or removes the amount parameter in the URL to reflect the current selection.
7) After a successful call to addGiftCardToCart, the page may update the URL to include cartId (and optionally addedGiftCardId) so that subsequent navigation (e.g., "Proceed to Checkout") can pass this cartId to cart_checkout.html via the query string.
8) No localStorage or other persistent browser storage is used; all detailed state beyond URL parameters is kept in memory for the duration of the page session.

components.functionality: On page load (and on URL parameter changes), fetches all available gift card products using getGiftCardProducts, resolves which GiftCardProduct should be initially selected based on productId and deliveryMethod URL parameters, and triggers loading of detailed data for the selected product.

Displays delivery method options and associated gift card products derived from the list returned by getGiftCardProducts. When the user selects a delivery method or a specific product, it updates the selected productId in state and the URL, then signals the detail loader to call getGiftCardProductDetail for that product.

Given the currently selected giftCardProductId, calls getGiftCardProductDetail to retrieve detailed configuration such as preset_amounts and min/max custom amounts. Provides this data to the amount selector and summary components.

Presents amount choices for the selected GiftCardProduct based on preset_amounts and allows entry of a custom amount within min_custom_amount and max_custom_amount. Applies URL parameter 'amount' on initialization for preselection. Ensures that a preset and custom amount cannot both be active simultaneously, and exposes validation state for use by the submit handler.

Collects recipient_name, recipient_email, sender_name, sender_email, and an optional personal_message for the gift card. Maintains an in-memory representation of the GiftCard configuration (excluding ID and status, which are set by the backend) and exposes field-level validation results (e.g., required fields, email format).

Displays a summary of the current configuration (selected product name, delivery_method, selected amount, recipient and sender names) and contains the "Add Gift Card to Cart" button. On submit, validates that all required data from the amount selector and recipient/sender form is present and valid, then calls addGiftCardToCart with giftCardProductId, amount, deliveryMethod (from the selected product), recipientName, recipientEmail, senderName, senderEmail, and personalMessage. Handles the response by showing success or error messages, storing the returned Cart, GiftCard, and CartItem in state, and exposing the "Proceed to Checkout" action that navigates to cart_checkout.html with cartId in the URL when provided.

Shows validation errors (e.g., missing fields, invalid amount range, email format problems) and API errors returned by addGiftCardToCart or getGiftCardProductDetail. Also displays a confirmation or success message when a gift card has been successfully added to the cart, optionally referencing the most recent GiftCard and CartItem in state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: Keep the site-wide utility bar, header, and full-width hero consistent with the mockup. The hero shows a workshop/garage image with a bold left-aligned 'Gift Cards' title in white. Directly overlapping the lower edge of the hero, a centered max-width content container holds a two-column layout: the left column is a light, semi-transparent configuration panel for all gift card form elements, while the right column holds the product image/preview, pricing summary, and add-to-cart actions. Notifications sit just under the hero but above the main two-column panel, maintaining a clear top-to-bottom reading order.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Keep the site-wide utility bar, header, and full-width hero consistent with the mockup. The hero shows a workshop/garage image with a bold left-aligned 'Gift Cards' title in white. Directly overlapping the lower edge of the hero, a centered max-width content container holds a two-column layout: the left column is a light, semi-transparent configuration panel for all gift card form elements, while the right column holds the product image/preview, pricing summary, and add-to-cart actions. Notifications sit just under the hero but above the main two-column panel, maintaining a clear top-to-bottom reading order.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly 10–15% of the viewport height is reserved for the utility bar and header, 25–35% for the hero, and the remaining initial viewport height for the overlapping two-column gift card configurator. The main content container is centered with generous side margins (~80–120px at large breakpoints), using a 12-column grid where the left configuration column takes about 7/12 of the width and the right summary/preview column about 5/12. Internal sections within the left column are separated by 24–32px vertical spacing and padded with 16–24px, adhering to the 8px spacing scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is moderately dense and brochure-like: form fields and selectors are grouped into clear sections with comfortable line heights and 16–24px padding per block. There is enough whitespace between sections (24–32px) so the forms feel approachable, not cramped. The right column uses a large image/preview and a compact but legible summary block, balancing the more text-heavy form on the left.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the top-left logo and main navigation, then moves across the top bar, down to the left-aligned 'Gift Cards' hero heading. From there it drops into the overlapping left configuration column: first the delivery method/product selector, then amount selection, then recipient/sender details. Only after scanning and interacting with the left column does the eye move to the right-hand preview and summary, which serves as a confirmation and call to action. Feedback messages appear near the top of the main content area, ensuring they are seen before users focus deeply on the form. The overall flow is an F-pattern, with left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading, subtly reinforced by the strong blue and yellow brand colors.

layout.overall_description: Below the existing yellow utility bar and white header, a full-width hero section shows a workshop or building-signage photo. On the left-middle of this hero image, the page title 'Gift Cards' appears in bold white text, mirroring the 'Services Provided' treatment from the mockup. The hero occupies about the top third of the viewport. Overlapping its lower edge, a centered max-width content container (aligned to a 12-column grid) introduces the main gift card configurator. A light, slightly translucent panel rises from the bottom of the hero and forms the left two-thirds of the content: this vertical panel holds all selection and form steps, stacked in logical order with clear headings and comfortable spacing. To the right, aligned to the container’s right edge, a solid panel containing the gift card preview, pricing summary, and a strong add-to-cart button anchors the layout, visually echoing the mechanic photo placement in the mockup. Notifications appear as slim bars just under the hero and above the overlapping panels. Together, these pieces create a clear, corporate-feeling configuration flow with a familiar workshop backdrop and a practical, no-frills aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active, this page-level loader appears as a semi-transparent white overlay that covers the entire main content area below the site header, including the hero and the two-column configurator. It does not alter the position of other components but temporarily sits above them, centered horizontally and vertically within the viewport. A simple spinner or progress indicator is placed in the middle of the screen, with a subtle dimming of the workshop/hero image visible behind it. Once initial data is loaded, this overlay disappears completely, revealing the hero at the top and the overlapping two-column layout beneath.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the full viewport area below the global header while loading; otherwise not rendered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This selector is positioned at the very top of the left configuration panel that overlaps the bottom portion of the hero. The left panel occupies roughly 7/12 of the max-width container and sits slightly inset from the hero’s left edge, with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. The selector spans nearly the full width of this left column, leaving a small margin (16px) to the panel edges. Horizontally, a heading like 'Choose Delivery Method' sits at the top-left of this block, with two or three pill-style options (e.g., Digital, Physical) arranged in a horizontal row just below, aligned to the left but not stretching edge-to-edge. If specific gift card designs are selectable, small card thumbnails are placed in a second row beneath the delivery method, aligned in a grid of 2–3 columns. There is ~24px of vertical space below this selector before the next form section, maintaining a clear sectional break while keeping the overall configuration flow tight and linear.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it anchors the top of the main form stack and shifts downward only as content above (such as notifications) appears.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This loader occupies the upper part of the right-hand column where the gift card preview and summary will appear. The right column itself takes about 5/12 of the container width and is aligned flush to the right edge of the max-width grid. Within this column, the loader appears as a rounded-rectangle skeleton block centered horizontally, spanning roughly 80–90% of the column width and about the height of a typical card image plus its summary (roughly the top half of the right column above the add-to-cart area). The skeleton has 16–24px padding inside its bounds and is vertically aligned near the top of the right column so that it visually pairs with the top of the left configuration panel, echoing the balanced composition of the original mechanic photo and text. Once the product data loads, this skeleton is replaced by the actual preview and summary content but retains the same spatial footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static placeholder within the right column; shown only while product details are loading and then replaced in place by the final content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Directly beneath the delivery-method selector in the left configuration column, the amount selector forms the next stacked block. It is left-aligned and uses the full usable width of the column minus standard side padding, matching the width of the selector above to create a unified vertical rail. A subsection heading such as 'Select Amount' sits at the top-left, followed by a grid or row of preset amount buttons (e.g., $50, $100, $200), arranged in one or two rows. Each amount button is a flat, rectangular pill with equal height and consistent spacing (8–16px) between them. Below the presets, an optional 'Custom amount' input stretches to nearly the full column width. The block is padded with about 16–24px internally and separated from the section above and below by 24–32px of vertical space, keeping the forms visually grouped but not crowded. This section reaches roughly 20–25% of the vertical height of the left column above the fold, depending on the number of options.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, participating in the natural top-to-bottom flow of form sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This form occupies the lower portion of the left configuration column and is visually the largest text block in the configurator. It begins immediately below the amount selector, with ~24px of vertical separation, and extends downward toward the bottom of the initial viewport. The form width matches the other left-column blocks, using the full internal width with 16–24px side padding. A section heading such as 'Recipient Details' is placed at the top-left, followed by stacked input fields (name, email, message, sender name, etc.) arranged in a single column. Where appropriate, pairs of smaller related fields (e.g., first name/last name) may sit side-by-side, each taking about 48–50% of the available width with 8–16px between them, still left-aligned. Fields are separated vertically by 12–16px, with slightly more space (24px) between logically distinct groups (recipient vs sender). This form creates a strong vertical block that visually balances against the tall product/summary area on the right, mirroring the long text block from 'Logbook Servicing' in the original mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, forming the main body of the left column; it can extend below the fold and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary and submit section is the core element of the right column and is visually positioned to align with the main portion of the form on the left. In the right 5/12 column, directly under (or combined with) the product preview area, a card-like block spans almost the full column width (with 16–24px padding and small outer margins). At the top of this block, a concise title such as 'Gift Card Summary' appears, followed by a stacked list of key details (delivery method, amount, recipient) with clear labels and values, each separated by 8–12px. Beneath the summary lines, the total price is emphasized with larger or bolder text, left-aligned. The primary 'Add to Cart' button is placed prominently at the bottom of this block, stretching close to full width of the summary card, with generous vertical padding to make it easy to tap/click. There is a small amount of space (16–24px) between the bottom of this block and the lower edge of the hero overlap area, making the right panel feel anchored but not cramped. Overall, this section occupies roughly the upper two-thirds of the right column above the fold, serving as the main action focal point in the F-pattern’s right side.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, anchored in the upper portion of the right column; it scrolls with the page and does not stick, preserving the straightforward corporate layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Feedback messages are displayed as a slim horizontal bar or stack of bars within the main content container, directly below the hero and just above the overlapping two-column configuration panel. They span the full width of the content container (not the full browser width), aligning with the left and right edges of the two columns. When visible, a message bar is 40–64px tall, with 16px internal padding and a left-aligned icon plus message text. Success messages may use a subtle green accent line or icon, while errors may use a red accent, but the background remains light to fit the clean, corporate aesthetic. The presence of a message shifts the two-column panel downward by its own height, preserving the spacing between elements and maintaining the sense that feedback is contextual to the entire gift card configuration. When no messages are present, this area collapses fully, leaving the hero and the overlapping panel snugly adjacent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a dedicated horizontal slot between the hero and the main configurator; appears and disappears without overlapping other components.

name: Cart & Checkout
title: Cart & Checkout
description: Unified page to review cart contents, adjust items, and complete checkout for services, maintenance plans, and gift cards.

core_features: Load and display full cart contents, including services, maintenance plans, and gift cards, using getCartSummary.
Allow users to adjust cart items, including updating quantities via updateCartItemQuantity and removing items via removeCartItem.
Display an order summary with subtotal, discounts, taxes, and total using data from getCartSummary and getCheckoutSummary.
Provide a checkout step that loads a checkout-ready summary with billing requirements and supported payment methods using getCheckoutSummary.
Collect billing and payment data in a checkout form and finalize the purchase/booking via submitCheckout.
Support multiple entry modes (cart review, checkout, confirmation) controlled via URL parameters, without relying on local storage.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View and review cart items
1. User navigates to cart_checkout.html (e.g., by clicking the header cart icon or a 'View Cart' link).
2. Page reads URL parameter step. If step is not provided or is 'cart', it sets current mode to 'cart'.
3. The cart-checkout router component calls getCartSummary.
4. The cart items list displays each CartItem with associated details (service package, maintenance plan, gift card, coupon, location, appointment) from the getCartSummary response.
5. The summary panel displays subtotal, discount_total, tax_total, and total from cart.
6. If the cart is empty, an empty-cart state is shown instead of the item list, and checkout actions are disabled.

Workflow: Update item quantity in cart
1. User is in step=cart mode on cart_checkout.html and sees the item list loaded from getCartSummary.
2. User changes the quantity control for a specific cart item (e.g., increment, decrement, or direct input).
3. The cart item actions component validates that the quantity is a positive integer and triggers updateCartItemQuantity with parameters cartItemId and the new quantity.
4. On success, the component receives the updated cart and items from the updateCartItemQuantity response.
5. The router updates local page state with the new cart and items, re-renders the item list and the summary panel, and totals update using the returned cart values.
6. If the API returns an error, an inline error message is shown for that item, and the quantity input reverts to the last known valid value.

Workflow: Remove an item from the cart
1. User is in step=cart mode and sees the item list.
2. User clicks the remove control for a specific cart item.
3. The cart item actions component triggers removeCartItem with parameter cartItemId.
4. On success, the component receives the updated cart and items from the removeCartItem response.
5. The item list is re-rendered without the removed item; the summary panel is updated with new subtotal, discounts, taxes, and total.
6. If there are no remaining items, the empty-cart state is displayed, and the proceed-to-checkout action is disabled.
7. On error, an inline error message is shown, and the item remains visible.

Workflow: Proceed from cart to checkout
1. User is reviewing items in step=cart mode and clicks the primary 'Proceed to Checkout' action.
2. The router updates the URL to cart_checkout.html?step=checkout (preserving any existing relevant parameters like highlightItemId) using history.pushState or a redirect.
3. On entering step=checkout mode, the router calls getCheckoutSummary.
4. The checkout summary panel displays items and line_total values, along with the updated cart totals.
5. The checkout controller reads billing_required and supported_payment_methods from the getCheckoutSummary response.
6. If billing_required is true, the billing/contact form and payment method selector components are shown; otherwise they may be minimized or optional while still displaying totals.
7. The user can navigate back to cart by clicking a 'Back to Cart' control, which changes the URL back to step=cart and triggers a getCartSummary call.

Workflow: Select payment method and submit checkout
1. With step=checkout mode active and getCheckoutSummary loaded, the payment method selector displays available options based on supported_payment_methods.
2. User selects a payment method; the payment method selector integrates with the chosen payment provider to obtain a paymentMethodToken (outside the scope of this page's APIs).
3. User completes the billing contact form fields (billingContact structure) as required.
4. User clicks the 'Complete Purchase' or 'Confirm Booking' button.
5. The checkout submit handler validates the billingContact fields and verifies that a paymentMethodToken has been obtained if billing_required is true.
6. On successful validation, submitCheckout is called with paymentMethodToken and billingContact.
7. If submitCheckout returns success=true, the router updates URL to cart_checkout.html?step=confirmation&orderId={order_id} and updates page state with the returned cart and message.
8. The confirmation view is displayed, using cart and order_id from the submitCheckout response; cart.status should now be 'checked_out', so modification controls are disabled.
9. If submitCheckout returns success=false, an error message from message is displayed above the checkout form, and the user stays in step=checkout mode to correct issues.

Workflow: Access checkout directly from another page
1. User triggers an action like 'Proceed to Checkout' from another page (e.g., service booking flow) that navigates directly to cart_checkout.html?step=checkout.
2. On load, the router detects step=checkout and immediately calls getCheckoutSummary.
3. If the cart is empty or cart.status is not 'active', the router shows an appropriate message and may switch to step=cart view, calling getCartSummary to confirm state.
4. If there are items and the cart is active, the checkout view is displayed directly; user can still navigate back to cart via the 'Back to Cart' control.

Workflow: Refresh or revisit page
1. User refreshes cart_checkout.html while in any step mode, with current URL parameters preserved.
2. On reload, the router reads step from URL.
3. If step=cart, getCartSummary is called; if step=checkout, getCheckoutSummary is called; if step=confirmation, the page uses orderId from the URL for display context and can optionally call getCheckoutSummary to show final totals if cart is still available.
4. All displayed data (items, totals, billing requirements) are rebuilt entirely from API responses and URL parameters, not from local storage.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial page load
- Trigger: window load or route activation for cart_checkout.html.
- Behavior: Read URL parameters (e.g., step, highlightItemId, orderId, errorCode). If step is 'checkout', call getCheckoutSummary; if step is 'confirmation', display confirmation using existing data and orderId; otherwise default to 'cart' and call getCartSummary. Based on the response, render cart items, summary, and appropriate step view.

Interaction: Quantity change for a cart item
- Trigger: User changes quantity input for a CartItem (e.g., input blur, plus/minus click, or change event).
- Behavior: Validate quantity (>0). If valid, call updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId and quantity. While awaiting response, show a loading indicator for the affected row and disable its controls. On success, replace cart and items in state with the returned data and re-render the list and summary. On failure, show inline error and revert quantity input.

Interaction: Remove cart item
- Trigger: User clicks 'Remove' on a cart item.
- Behavior: Optionally confirm removal in the UI; on confirmation, call removeCartItem with cartItemId. While awaiting response, show loading state on that row. On success, update state with the returned cart and items; re-render. If items array becomes empty, show the empty-cart component and disable checkout-related actions. On failure, show inline error and keep the item.

Interaction: Proceed to checkout from cart
- Trigger: User clicks 'Proceed to Checkout' when in step=cart.
- Behavior: Update URL to include step=checkout (preserving other known parameters such as highlightItemId if present). Switch internal mode to 'checkout', call getCheckoutSummary, and on success display checkout view: items with line_total, cart totals, billing form if billing_required, and payment options based on supported_payment_methods.

Interaction: Navigate back to cart from checkout
- Trigger: User clicks 'Back to Cart' from checkout view.
- Behavior: Update URL to step=cart. Switch internal mode to 'cart', call getCartSummary to refresh items in case of changes from other flows, and display cart view with modification controls.

Interaction: Select payment method
- Trigger: User selects one of the supported_payment_methods (e.g., a radio button).
- Behavior: The payment selector configures or initializes the corresponding payment provider logic. When the provider flow completes successfully, it sets or updates a paymentMethodToken in local page state. The selected method and token are required inputs for submitCheckout, but the token itself is not persisted outside this page.

Interaction: Submit checkout
- Trigger: User clicks the primary 'Complete Purchase' / 'Confirm Booking' button.
- Behavior: The checkout form validates required billingContact fields and checks that paymentMethodToken is present when billing_required is true. If validation fails, validation errors are shown and the submit is blocked. On success, call submitCheckout with paymentMethodToken and billingContact. While waiting, show a global loading state and disable the submit button. If the response has success=true, update URL to step=confirmation&orderId={order_id}, store returned cart, and show confirmation view. If success=false, display message and keep the user in checkout view.

Interaction: Confirmation view usage
- Trigger: User arrives with URL parameter step=confirmation (either immediately after submitCheckout success, or by revisiting a confirmation link with orderId in URL).
- Behavior: Confirmation view displays order_id and final cart summary (using last known cart from submitCheckout or from a fresh getCheckoutSummary call if cart is still active in the backend). All cart modification controls are disabled if cart.status is not 'active'. If necessary, an informational message is shown using message from submitCheckout.

Interaction: Highlight a specific cart item
- Trigger: Page is loaded or navigated to with highlightItemId in the URL (e.g., cart_checkout.html?step=cart&highlightItemId={id}).
- Behavior: After getCartSummary returns, the cart items list searches for a CartItem with id equal to highlightItemId and applies a visual and/or positional focus (e.g., scroll into view, highlight background). No extra API calls are performed. If the item does not exist, no highlight is applied.

Interaction: Handle inactive or checked-out cart
- Trigger: Any data load (getCartSummary or getCheckoutSummary) returns cart.status that is not 'active'.
- Behavior: The router disables cart modification and checkout actions. If status is 'checked_out', a message explains that the cart has already been processed and may prompt the user to start a new session via navigation outside this page.
