component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state occupies the same central content column inside the about-page-controller as the loading state, appearing when data fails to load. It is positioned with a top margin of about 32–48px from the bottom of the hero or header area, so the error message appears clearly beneath the site’s main chrome. The error block itself is a compact card spanning about 50–60% of the content width on large screens, centered horizontally. Inside, the message text is left-aligned with comfortable padding (around 16–24px on all sides), and any retry button or link is aligned to the left below the message, preserving the F-pattern orientation. There is ample whitespace below the error card (at least 64px) to keep it visually isolated. While this state is active, the normal About sections (navigation, Overview, Mission, lists, CTA) are hidden, so the error card becomes the sole focal element in the About content zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in place of other content within the normal flow. It does not float or stick but occupies the main scroll position; in most error cases, the page height will be limited to just this message and the global header/hero above.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The section navigation is the first persistent About-specific element visible beneath the hero and is anchored at the top of the about-page-controller. It appears as a slim horizontal bar spanning about 70–80% of the content width, centered within the max-width container. Vertically, it sits roughly 32px below the bottom edge of the hero image (or directly under any hero overlap), and about 24–32px above the top of the Overview card. The nav items (e.g., Overview, Mission, Certifications, Guarantees) are laid out in a row from left to right, aligned to the center horizontally but with a slight left bias in item emphasis to maintain the F-pattern. Each item has comfortable horizontal spacing (16–24px between labels) and minimal vertical height, so the overall bar feels slim and utility-like. On smaller screens, this row can become horizontally scrollable but remains pinned beneath the hero and above the sections. The background of this bar is either white or a very light neutral, keeping it visually subordinate to the hero yet clearly separated via a subtle bottom border or shadow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sits in normal document flow beneath the hero; not fixed or sticky by default, so it scrolls out of view as the user scrolls down through the sections. It serves primarily as anchor links for in-page navigation to each About subsection.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The About Overview section is the first major content block and visually the most prominent within the About area, echoing the mockup’s overlapping text panel. On large screens, it appears as a light-colored card that slightly overlaps the bottom of the hero image above (for example, its top edge may be pulled up with a negative margin of about 16–24px into the hero’s lower boundary), reinforcing the layered effect. Horizontally, the card is aligned to the left side of the 12-column grid, occupying roughly 6–7 columns (about 55–60% of the content width), with the right side left open as breathing space that visually recalls where an image might sit in the original design. The card has generous internal padding (around 24px on all sides) and contains the main 'About Us' overview heading and several paragraphs of narrative text. The text is left-aligned, with paragraph spacing of about 12–16px to maintain readability. The bottom edge of this card sits about 32–40px above the Mission section, maintaining the clear vertical rhythm. On tablet and mobile, this section becomes a full-width block centered in the container, losing the physical overlap with the hero if necessary but retaining a slightly elevated feel through subtle shadow or border.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the normal flow but with a visual overlap toward the hero above at larger breakpoints, achieved using negative margin or positioning. It does not scroll separately and resizes responsively from a left-side card on desktop to a full-width block on smaller screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Mission section is positioned directly below the Overview card, separated by a consistent vertical gap of around 32–40px. It spans a wider portion of the grid than the Overview—approximately 9–10 of the 12 columns (around 75–85% of the content width)—but remains centered inside the main container, with its left edge aligned with the left edge of the Overview card to maintain a clean column. This creates the sense of a continuous left-aligned text spine as the user scrolls. Inside, the mission heading sits at the top left of the block, followed by one or more paragraphs of narrative text. Internal padding (16–24px) matches the Overview, and line-height remains comfortable, preserving the moderate content density. Visually, this section feels like a stable, full-width text zone that anchors the middle of the page, with enough negative space above and below to avoid overwhelming the user. The right side of this block aligns with the container’s right margin, differentiating it subtly from the narrower Overview card above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level section in the document flow with no overlapping behavior. It expands and contracts with the container, becoming full-width on smaller screens while keeping text left-aligned.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Certifications list forms the left column of a two-column band that appears below the Mission section, separated from it by a vertical gap of about 32–48px. The entire band sits within the same max-width container, but inside that band, the Certifications card occupies the left 6 columns of the 12-column grid (roughly 50% of the content width). Its top edge aligns horizontally with the top of the Guarantees card on the right. The card has a clear heading ('Certifications') at the top left, followed by a vertically stacked list of items. Each list item is separated by 8–12px of vertical spacing, keeping the list readable and lightly structured rather than dense. The card uses around 16–24px of internal padding and a light background or border to visually group the items. This left-hand list is the first element encountered in the left-to-right scan of the band, mirroring the way the mockup’s text column precedes the right-side image. On mobile, this card stacks above the Guarantees list, maintaining a 24–32px gap between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in a shared horizontal row with the Guarantees list at larger breakpoints, forming a flexible two-column layout. It remains in normal flow and simply stacks vertically above the Guarantees card on narrower screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Guarantees list sits directly to the right of the Certifications list within the same horizontal band, occupying the right 6 columns of the 12-column grid (about 50% of the content width). Its top edge aligns with the Certifications card so both cards read as a cohesive, balanced pair similar to the mockup’s side-by-side text and image. The Guarantees card mirrors the styling of the Certifications card: a heading at the top left, followed by a list of guarantees with 8–12px vertical spacing between items, and internal padding of 16–24px. The background, border, or slight elevation is consistent with the Certifications card to visually tie them together. This right column provides a secondary but important focal area in the F-pattern, drawing attention after the user reads down the left column. The horizontal gap between the two cards is modest (about 24px), ensuring enough breathing room while still feeling like a single composite section. On mobile, this card appears directly below the Certifications card with a vertical gap of about 24–32px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Shares a two-column row with the Certifications list at large breakpoints and drops into a stacked layout below it on smaller screens. It remains in normal document flow and resizes responsively with the grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The Find Location CTA is the final section in the About page flow, placed below the Certifications/Guarantees band with a vertical gap of about 48px, signaling a transition from informational content to action. It appears as a full-width horizontal band within the max-width container, spanning nearly all 12 columns (roughly 90–100% of the content width) and centered horizontally. The band uses the brand’s stronger blue or yellow accent color to stand out against the more neutral sections above. Inside the band, a short line of inviting text is centered horizontally and vertically near the top, with the primary CTA button (e.g., 'Find a Location') centered directly beneath or beside the text. The button is relatively large, with generous padding (12–16px vertically, 24–32px horizontally), and sits in the lower half of the band’s height, ensuring it is the focal point. The band has taller vertical padding (32–40px top and bottom) than the other sections, producing a clear sense of importance and breathing room. This section leads naturally down into the site’s global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard full-width section in the flow; it anchors the bottom of the About content without any fixed or sticky behavior. On smaller screens, the text and button remain center-aligned and stack vertically with consistent spacing, maintaining clear tap targets.

name: Contact & Support
title: Contact & Support
description: Provides global contact information and a form to submit general support inquiries, including guidance related to appointments.

core_features: Fetch and display global contact and support information (phone, email, support hours, and appointment change instructions) using getContactSupportInfo.
Provide a general Contact & Support form for users to submit inquiries that are not covered by booking or quote flows, using submitContactSupportRequest.
Prefill and filter the contact form based on URL parameters (e.g., topic, name, email, phone) to streamline user input and support deep links from other pages.
Display contextual guidance on modifying or canceling appointments, sourced from appointment_change_instructions returned by getContactSupportInfo.
Show submission status and ticket information after a support request is sent, based on the response from submitContactSupportRequest and optional URL parameters.
user_workflows:
View contact details and support guidance:
1. User navigates to contact.html (e.g., via footer link).
2. On page load, the page calls getContactSupportInfo with no parameters.
3. The response data (support_phone, support_email, support_hours, appointment_change_instructions) is bound to the contact information and guidance components.
4. The user can see how to reach support and how to modify or cancel appointments based on the returned information.

Submit a general support inquiry:
1. User navigates to contact.html, optionally with URL parameters such as topic, name, email, or phone.
2. The page initializes the contact form, pre-populating fields from URL parameters when present (e.g., topic from ?topic=..., email from ?email=...).
3. User fills in or edits name, email, phone, topic, and message fields.
4. User submits the form, triggering submitContactSupportRequest with the current field values.
5. The page displays a loading state for the form submission while waiting for the API response.
6. On success, the page shows a confirmation message that includes the ticket_id and/or API message; optionally, the URL is updated (e.g., ?success=true&ticket_id=...) so the user can refresh or share the confirmation state.
7. On failure, the page shows an error message and leaves the form populated so the user can correct or retry.

Contact support specifically about an existing appointment (from another page):
1. User is on another page (e.g., appointment details) and clicks a link or button like "Contact Support" that navigates to contact.html with URL parameters (e.g., contact.html?topic=appointment&message=...pre-filled-text...).
2. On load, contact.html reads the URL parameters and pre-populates the topic and message fields (and any other provided fields such as name/email/phone).
3. User reviews or adds more details to the message.
4. User submits the form; submitContactSupportRequest is called with the prefilled and edited values.
5. The user receives a ticket_id via the success response and sees the confirmation on the page.

Return to Contact & Support to view submission confirmation:
1. After a successful submission, the page may update the URL (e.g., contact.html?success=true&ticket_id=...&topic=...).
2. If the user reloads or revisits this URL, the page reads the URL parameters.
3. When ?success=true is present, the page displays a persistent confirmation banner using the provided ticket_id and optional message text.
4. The form can remain visible (with fields cleared or prefilled from URL parameters depending on implementation) while still showing the confirmation state.
interactions: On page load, trigger getContactSupportInfo and render the returned support_phone, support_email, support_hours, and appointment_change_instructions in the contact information and guidance areas.

On URL parameter change (via navigation), re-initialize form fields based on query parameters such as topic, name, email, phone, and message, and update the success/error state based on parameters like success and ticket_id.
On user input into the contact form fields (name, email, phone, topic, message), update the internal form state that will be sent to submitContactSupportRequest.
On contact form submission, validate required fields on the client side (e.g., presence and basic format validations) before calling submitContactSupportRequest.

On successful submitContactSupportRequest response, display a success confirmation section that includes the returned ticket_id and message; optionally clear or lock the form fields and update the URL parameters to reflect success state.
On failed submitContactSupportRequest response, display an error notification and keep existing form values so the user can modify inputs and retry.

On explicit user action to dismiss success/error banners (e.g., clicking a close button), update the local UI state and optionally remove related URL parameters (e.g., remove success and ticket_id) to avoid showing outdated messages on refresh.

On explicit user action to copy or reference contact details (e.g., clicking a mailto or tel link), trigger the appropriate navigation/action using support_email or support_phone from getContactSupportInfo.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page relies on URL query parameters to manage and restore state. The base page is contact.html. State is derived as follows:

1) Prefilling form fields from URL parameters:
- If ?name= is present, its value initializes the Name field in the contact form.
- If ?email= is present, its value initializes the Email field in the contact form.
- If ?phone= is present, its value initializes the Phone field in the contact form.
- If ?topic= is present, its value initializes the Topic field in the contact form (e.g., as a pre-selected option or text value).
- If ?message= is present, its value initializes the Message textarea in the contact form.
These prefilled values can be edited by the user; they are used only as initial state on load or when the URL changes.

2) Submission result and ticket state:
- If ?success=true is present, the page displays a submission success banner/section, even on reload, using the optional ticket_id and message parameters if provided.
- If ?success=false is present, the page may show a generic error banner indicating that a support request did not complete successfully.
- If ?ticket_id= is present alongside ?success=true, the confirmation section includes this ticket identifier for user reference.
- If ?api_message= is present, it may be used to display a user-facing description of the result (e.g., as returned from submitContactSupportRequest.message), assuming it has been URL-encoded safely.

3) Derived internal state (not stored outside the page):
- The page maintains a transient 'loading' state while getContactSupportInfo is in progress; during this time, contact details and guidance components may show a loading indicator, and actions that rely on this data should be disabled.
- The page maintains a transient 'submitting' state for the contact form while submitContactSupportRequest is in progress; during this time, the submit button is disabled and a progress indicator is shown.
- Error states (for either getContactSupportInfo or submitContactSupportRequest) are represented in the UI but are not persisted in the URL unless explicitly encoded via parameters as described above.

4) Navigation and deep-link behavior:
- Other pages can construct deep links into contact.html with prefilled context, for example including topic, name, email, phone, and message to reduce user typing.
- After a successful submission, the page may update the URL to include success and ticket_id parameters (e.g., using history.pushState or a redirect) so that the confirmation can be revisited by reloading the page.
- If the user manually edits the URL parameters and reloads, the page will reflect those parameters in the form and confirmation states without relying on any stored session or localStorage data.

components.functionality: On initial page load (and optionally on explicit refresh), calls getContactSupportInfo to retrieve global support contact details and instructions, and exposes this data to other components.
Displays current global support contact details (phone, email, hours) and provides actionable links (e.g., click-to-call, click-to-email) based on data from getContactSupportInfo.
Shows textual guidance on how users can modify or cancel appointments and where to find location-specific contact information, using appointment_change_instructions from getContactSupportInfo.
Reads URL parameters (such as name, email, phone, topic, message, success, ticket_id, api_message) on page load or navigation, and initializes the form fields and submission status state accordingly.
Provides input fields for name, email, phone, topic, and message; validates user input; and on submission calls submitContactSupportRequest with the collected values.

Handles the submission logic: when the contact-support-form is submitted and passes validation, calls submitContactSupportRequest with name, email, phone, topic, and message; manages submitting/loading state and interprets the API response to update success or error state.

Displays success or error messages related to contact form submissions, including the ticket_id and any descriptive message from submitContactSupportRequest or URL parameters. It persists visible success state when ?success=true is present in the URL.

Keeps relevant state in sync with the URL: after a successful submission, it can add or update success, ticket_id, topic, and api_message parameters; when a banner is dismissed, it can remove success- and ticket-related parameters to avoid showing outdated messages on refresh.
If getContactSupportInfo fails, this component shows an error message and presents an action to retry loading support information without relying on cached or static values.
Shows or hides loading indicators based on the status of getContactSupportInfo and submitContactSupportRequest, without altering any underlying data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global header and nav at the very top, followed by a full-width hero/banner image specific to the Contact & Support page with the main title placed in the left-middle. Directly below the hero, a single main content section uses a two-column composition: the left column is a light, semi-transparent information panel containing contact details and appointment guidance; the right column contains the contact/support form. This section visually overlaps the lower part of the hero image, echoing the layered effect from the mockup. Logic-only components (loaders, handlers, URL sync) sit behind these visual elements and do not disrupt the grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: global header and nav at the very top, followed by a full-width hero/banner image specific to the Contact & Support page with the main title placed in the left-middle. Directly below the hero, a single main content section uses a two-column composition: the left column is a light, semi-transparent information panel containing contact details and appointment guidance; the right column contains the contact/support form. This section visually overlaps the lower part of the hero image, echoing the layered effect from the mockup. Logic-only components (loaders, handlers, URL sync) sit behind these visual elements and do not disrupt the grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and utility bar together consume around the top 10–15% of the viewport. The hero image below them uses roughly 25–35% of the initial viewport height, with the page title positioned on the left over the image. The overlapping contact-and-form section fills the remaining initial viewport height: the left information panel occupies about 55–60% of the content width, and the right form card occupies about 40–45%. Inside each panel, content breathing room follows 16–24px padding on all sides. A max-width centered container (around 1100–1200px) is used for the main content, with generous horizontal margins (~80–120px from the viewport edges on large screens). Vertical gaps between major sections align to the 32–48px scale.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and brochure-like. The contact information and appointment guidance use comfortable line-height and 16–24px vertical spacing between paragraphs or groups. The form fields are stacked with consistent 16px vertical spacing, label-to-field spacing around 8px, and clear section breaks where appropriate. There is enough whitespace within both the left information panel and the right form card so neither feels cramped, balancing the heavy text with the strong hero image and clean blocks of color.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F-pattern, starting at the top-left logo and nav, then scanning across the top. The eye is next pulled down to the left-aligned hero title ('Contact & Support'). From there, the gaze drops into the overlapping left information panel, first absorbing the key contact details, then scrolling down through appointment guidance. Only after scanning the left stack does attention move to the right-hand form card, which serves as the main call-to-action area. When error or status banners appear, they do so near the top of their respective columns, so the eye naturally catches them during this left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading flow.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar yellow utility bar and white navigation header, the Contact & Support page opens with a full-width hero photograph of the workshop exterior or signage, tinted slightly darker to ensure legibility. In the left-middle of this image, the page title 'Contact & Support' appears in large, bold white type that mirrors the 'Services Provided' hero treatment from the mockup. As you scroll just slightly, a light semi-transparent panel seems to float up from the bottom edge of the hero, overlapping it by about one third of its height. This panel is aligned to the left half of the centered content area and contains the business’s contact details at the top, with appointment guidance text flowing beneath. To its right, aligned with the same top baseline but slightly separated by white space, sits a solid white card holding the contact support form, framed by subtle borders and padding. Together, these two blocks form a balanced two-column layout that fills the rest of the first screen. System feedback like loading and error states appear inline within these panels or as a slim overlay, but never break the overall grid or hierarchy. On smaller screens, the overlapping effect softens into stacked sections: hero image and title at the top, followed by the full-width contact/guidance panel, and then the full-width form card beneath, maintaining the same calm, corporate workshop aesthetic throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual data-fetching layer that conceptually wraps the main content area beneath the hero. It initializes as soon as the user reaches the Contact & Support page and governs what appears in the left information column: while it is loading, the left column shows the page-loading-indicator; once data is fetched, it reveals the contact-info-panel and appointment-guidance-panel; if it fails, it swaps those panels out for the support-info-error-state. It does not occupy any visible space itself and does not alter the grid; instead, it controls which visual component is rendered in the left column’s footprint.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; behaves as an invisible wrapper around the left-column content region directly under the hero, controlling which child component occupies that region without affecting layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-panel lives in the upper portion of the left overlapping block beneath the hero. On desktop, it occupies roughly the left 55–60% of the max-width content container. Its background is a light, slightly transparent white or pale tone that allows a hint of the workshop hero image to show through, echoing the mockup’s semi-transparent text block. The top edge of this panel overlaps the bottom of the hero by about 64–96px, creating a layered effect. Inside, the panel has 24px padding on all sides. The key contact details (phone, address, email, opening hours) are arranged in short, clearly separated lines with 8–16px vertical spacing between each line. The panel’s right edge aligns vertically with the midline between the left and right columns, leaving a modest 32–48px gap before the form card begins. On tablet and mobile, this panel becomes full-width, sits directly below the hero with a 32px margin on top, and retains its padding while increasing vertical spacing slightly for readability.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; its vertical position is locked relative to the hero so that it consistently overlaps the lower hero edge on larger screens. It resizes responsively (from ~60% width to 100%) and pushes subsequent content in its column down as its content height grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The appointment-guidance-panel is visually and structurally attached to the bottom of the contact-info-panel, forming a single cohesive left-hand information block. It shares the same light semi-transparent background and width as the contact-info-panel, appearing as a continuation of the same card rather than a separate box. There is a subtle internal separation—such as a 16px vertical gap and possibly a soft divider line—between the last line of contact info and the beginning of the appointment guidance content. The guidance content consists of one or more paragraphs with 16–24px spacing between them, spanning the full internal width of the panel and maintaining 24px side padding. Overall, the combined height of the contact-info-panel plus appointment-guidance-panel creates a column that typically extends slightly further down the page than the form card, anchoring the left side of the layout. On smaller screens, this guidance content appears directly under the contact info in a full-width block, continuing the same background and padding with no horizontal separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; it expands vertically beneath the contact-info-panel within the same left column. Its height is content-driven, pushing the rest of the page downward as more guidance text is added, but its horizontal footprint stays locked to the left column (or full-width on mobile).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual state initializer for the contact-support-form. It resides logically in the same right column region as the form but does not render any UI of its own. When the page loads, it prepares default field values, validation state, and any URL-synced data before the form becomes interactive. It shares the same spatial footprint as the form card but does not add extra spacing; instead, it ensures the form is ready when the card is displayed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; executes before and alongside the form in the right column without affecting layout. It does not consume space or alter the positioning of visible elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-support-form is presented as a solid white card in the right column of the overlapping section, visually balancing the left information block. On large screens, it occupies about 40–45% of the content width, aligned to the right edge of the main container, with its top edge aligned roughly to the top of the left panel’s contact-info section (slightly lower than the hero’s bottom edge to preserve the overlapping effect primarily on the left side). The form card has 24px padding inside, a subtle border or drop shadow to stand off from the background, and rounded corners consistent with the site’s style. At the top of the card, there is room reserved for the submission-status-banner when it appears; otherwise, the first form heading or label starts 8–16px below the top padding. Form fields are stacked vertically, each spanning the full internal width of the card with 8px between label and input and 16px between each field group. The main submit button is aligned left within the card bottom, with 24px bottom padding below it. A gap of 32–48px separates this card from the left information block. On tablet, the card’s width increases to ~50–60% and centers more under the left column; on mobile, it becomes full-width and is stacked below the left panel, separated by a 32px vertical margin.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column; its width responds fluidly with the grid, but it remains a vertically flowing card. Height is determined by the number of fields and validation messages. When the page height is shorter than the content, the form scrolls naturally with the page rather than staying fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is a non-visual action handler tied to the contact-support-form. It lives conceptually inside the form card region and listens for submission events, triggering API calls and updating the submission-status-banner and form field states. It does not render any visible elements and therefore does not alter the spacing, padding, or alignment of the form card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; attached to the form’s submit behavior. Occupies no visual space and does not change layout positions, only the state of other visible components within the same right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The submission-status-banner appears inside the contact-support-form card at the top, spanning the full internal width of the card. When visible, it sits directly beneath the card’s top padding and pushes the form fields downward. It is a horizontal strip approximately 48–64px tall with 16px vertical padding, using a strong but brand-consistent background color (e.g., yellow for neutral/success, muted red for error) and clear, medium-weight text. The banner’s left edge aligns with the left padding of the form card, and the right edge aligns with the right padding, maintaining the same 24px inset from the card’s border. When not active, the space collapses so the form fields rise to immediately follow the card’s top padding. On smaller screens, the banner keeps the same full-width behavior, but text wraps cleanly within the card’s width, maintaining comfortable line-height.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form card; appears and disappears without overlaying content, instead pushing the fields below it down. It does not change the card’s width, only its height while visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual URL state manager runs alongside the contact-support-form and submission-status-banner, mapping parts of the URL (such as query parameters or hash fragments) to initial form values or status states (for example, preselecting an appointment topic or reflecting success via a status parameter). It has no visible representation in the layout and thus does not consume or change any space in the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; operates in the background tied to the page’s routing layer. It does not affect element positioning, only the initial state of visible components in the main content region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The support-info-error-state occupies the same left-column footprint as the contact-info-panel and appointment-guidance-panel when data loading fails. In that case, instead of the standard semi-transparent info block, a single error panel appears anchored in the same overlapping position beneath the hero, still covering roughly 55–60% of the content width on desktop. The panel retains similar padding (24px on all sides) and alignment, but uses an error-accented style—perhaps a white background with a strong red or orange header strip or icon—to clearly convey the issue. Its height is comparable to the usual contact+guidance content, but may be shorter if the message is brief, leaving additional whitespace below. A clear retry or alternative contact instruction is centered or left-aligned within the panel. On small screens, this error block becomes full-width directly below the hero, mirroring the behavior of the standard left information block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static replacement for the left information block; it takes over the same space as the contact-info-panel and appointment-guidance-panel without changing the rest of the layout. Appears within the same overlapping position beneath the hero and pushes lower content downward as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-loading-indicator appears whenever support or contact data is being fetched, before the main left and right content becomes fully interactive. It is composed of two visual elements: (1) a slim blue progress bar or loading strip running horizontally just under the main site header or at the very top edge of the hero, spanning 100% of the viewport width but only about 4px tall; and (2) a centered loading spinner or subtle animation placed in the center of the overlapping content region (between the left and right columns) while the data is loading. During this state, the areas where the contact-info-panel and contact-support-form would normally appear may show faint skeleton blocks or be lightly dimmed to indicate pending content, but the overall two-column spacing and hero layout remain intact. Once loading completes, the slim bar and spinner disappear, revealing the normal panels in their positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces content within the main content area without changing the underlying layout structure. The top loading bar is fixed to the top of the content area (just below the header) while active; the central spinner is centered within the visible portion of the overlapping section, not scrolling independently.

name: Privacy & Terms
title: Privacy & Terms
description: Displays dynamically loaded privacy policy and terms of service content, with URL-driven navigation between documents and sections.

core_features: Load and display the privacy policy content via getPrivacyPolicyContent, including its last_updated value and all sections.
Load and display the terms of service content via getTermsOfServiceContent, including its last_updated value and all sections.
Allow users to switch between the privacy policy and terms of service views using a tab-like control, with the selection reflected in URL parameters.
Generate a table of contents for the currently selected document from its sections array, enabling quick navigation to any section.
Support deep-linking to a specific document and section using URL parameters so users can share or bookmark exact locations within the policies.
Render HTML provided in body_html safely, including any embedded links to additional legal documents or disclosures.
Provide basic loading and error states for policy content retrieval, allowing users to retry loading if an interface call fails.
user_workflows: View privacy policy from footer: 1) User clicks the footer link pointing to policies.html (optionally with URL parameters). 2) Page initializes and the policy-page-loader component calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and getTermsOfServiceContent in parallel. 3) The policy-state-manager component reads URL parameters (e.g., document and section) to determine which document (privacy or terms) should be active on initial load. 4) Once content is loaded, the policy-tab-switcher highlights the active document, the policy-last-updated-banner shows the correct last_updated value for that document, the policy-toc lists its sections, and the policy-document-viewer scrolls to the section indicated by the section parameter if present. 5) The user reads the content and can click within the document or use the table of contents to navigate between sections.

Switch between privacy and terms: 1) While viewing one document, the user clicks the other document’s tab in the policy-tab-switcher. 2) The tab-switcher triggers an event to the policy-state-manager to update the document URL parameter to the selected document type. 3) The browser URL is updated via the History API without reloading the page (or a full reload uses the same parameters to restore the state). 4) The policy-toc updates to show sections for the newly active document, the policy-last-updated-banner shows its last_updated, and the policy-document-viewer switches to render its sections, resetting or updating the section parameter as defined by the state logic.

Navigate within a document via table of contents: 1) User clicks an entry in the policy-toc component. 2) The policy-toc emits an event with the target section identifier (derived from the section index or a slug based on section.heading). 3) The policy-state-manager updates the section URL parameter accordingly. 4) The policy-document-viewer listens for section changes, scrolls to the matching section anchor, and updates focus if needed for accessibility.

Open a deep link to a specific section: 1) User follows or enters a URL such as policies.html?document=privacy&section=<section-id>. 2) On load, the policy-state-manager parses document and section from the URL. 3) The policy-page-loader ensures both getPrivacyPolicyContent and getTermsOfServiceContent are called. 4) When loading completes, the active document is set from the document parameter, and the policy-toc and policy-document-viewer initialize accordingly. 5) The policy-document-viewer locates the section matching the section parameter and scrolls to or highlights it.

Handle loading or error states: 1) When the page first loads, the policy-page-loader sets loading flags for both documents and renders a loading state via dependent components. 2) If getPrivacyPolicyContent or getTermsOfServiceContent succeeds, the corresponding content is stored in shared state and loading flags clear. 3) If a call fails, the loader sets an error state for that document. 4) The policy-error-notice component shows an error message specific to the failed document and provides a retry control. 5) When the user activates retry, the loader calls the relevant interface again and updates the loading and error states based on the result.
interactions: Tab selection: Clicking a tab in the policy-tab-switcher changes the active document. This updates the document URL parameter, rebinds the active content for policy-last-updated-banner and policy-toc, and re-renders the policy-document-viewer for the selected document.

Table of contents click: Clicking a table of contents item in the policy-toc updates the section URL parameter and triggers the policy-document-viewer to scroll to the corresponding section and set focus to its heading.

In-document anchor navigation: Clicking links within body_html that reference in-page anchors (e.g., #<section-anchor>) scrolls to the relevant section; the policy-state-manager listens for hash or URL changes and synchronizes the section parameter to maintain state consistency.

External legal links: Clicking links within body_html that point to other pages or external legal documents follows standard browser navigation behavior. The policy-document-viewer does not alter these links and allows them to function as defined in body_html.

Retry loading content: If a document fails to load, clicking the retry control in the policy-error-notice component triggers the policy-page-loader to call the appropriate interface again, updating loading and error state while preserving current URL parameters.

URL navigation / back-forward: Using the browser’s back and forward buttons changes the query parameters in the URL. The policy-state-manager listens for history events, re-parses the document and section parameters, and updates the active tab, table of contents, and visible section accordingly without requiring new interface calls if content is already cached in memory.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL query parameters to control which document is active and which section is in focus. The primary parameters are: 1) document: defines the active document view. Expected values are mapped to internal document types (for example, a value representing the privacy policy vs. a value representing the terms of service). When absent or invalid, an internal default document type is used. 2) section: identifies the currently focused section within the active document. This may be the zero-based index of the section in the sections array from the relevant interface, or a slug derived from the section.heading using a deterministic slugification algorithm (e.g., lowercasing and replacing non-alphanumeric characters with dashes). On initial load, the policy-state-manager parses document and section. It sets the active document, then waits until both getPrivacyPolicyContent and getTermsOfServiceContent have completed. Once the active document content is available, the manager checks whether the section parameter corresponds to a valid section (by index or slug). If valid, it instructs the policy-document-viewer to scroll to that section; if not valid, it clears or adjusts the section parameter to a default (such as the first section) and updates the URL to maintain consistency. When the user interacts with the page, state updates feed back into the URL. Changing tabs updates the document parameter and resets or preserves the section parameter according to the rules defined (for example, clearing the section when switching documents or attempting to map a section index if the new document has at least that many sections). Clicking a table of contents entry sets the section parameter to the chosen section identifier. The policy-state-manager uses the History API (e.g., pushState/replaceState) to update query parameters without causing a full page reload. If the browser performs a full reload (such as when the user reloads the page manually or shares the URL), the same parameters are parsed again on initialization to restore the same visible document and section.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getPrivacyPolicyContent and getTermsOfServiceContent, stores their results in shared state, manages loading and error flags for each document, and exposes the content to other components.
event_handlers: onInit: Called when the page is first loaded; invokes getPrivacyPolicyContent and getTermsOfServiceContent in parallel and updates internal loading and error state.
onReloadPrivacy: Triggered when the user retries loading the privacy policy; calls getPrivacyPolicyContent and updates state accordingly.
onReloadTerms: Triggered when the user retries loading the terms of service; calls getTermsOfServiceContent and updates state accordingly.

components.functionality: Central controller that interprets URL parameters, determines the active document and section, coordinates updates between components, and keeps the browser URL in sync with user actions.
event_handlers: onURLParse: Runs on initial page load and on browser history events; reads query parameters to set activeDocument and activeSection state.
onDocumentChange: Receives document change events from policy-tab-switcher; updates activeDocument state and modifies the document query parameter using the History API.
onSectionChange: Receives section selection events from policy-toc or policy-document-viewer; updates activeSection state and modifies the section query parameter.

onHistoryChange: Listens to browser popstate events; re-parses query parameters and updates activeDocument and activeSection for all dependent components without re-calling the interfaces if content is already available.

components.functionality: Allows the user to select which document (privacy policy or terms of service) is currently active and visible.
event_handlers: onClickPrivacyTab: Triggered when the user selects the privacy policy tab; emits a document change event to policy-state-manager to set the active document to the privacy policy and update the document URL parameter.

onClickTermsTab: Triggered when the user selects the terms of service tab; emits a document change event to policy-state-manager to set the active document to the terms of service and update the document URL parameter.

components.functionality: Displays the last_updated value for the currently active document as provided by the relevant interface.
event_handlers: onActiveDocumentChange: When activeDocument changes, the banner updates to show the last_updated value of the corresponding document.
onContentLoaded: When either interface completes successfully, the banner checks if that document is active and updates the displayed last_updated accordingly.

components.functionality: Generates and displays a clickable table of contents for the currently active document, using its sections array to create a list of entries that navigate to individual sections.
event_handlers: onActiveDocumentChange: When the active document changes, rebuilds the table of contents from that document’s sections.
onSectionClick: When the user clicks a table of contents item, emits a section change event with the section identifier to policy-state-manager to update the section URL parameter.
onActiveSectionChange: Highlights the corresponding table of contents item when the active section changes, providing visual feedback about the current section.

components.functionality: Renders the sections of the active document in sequence, using each section.heading and section.body_html, and exposes anchors or identifiers that allow scrolling to specific sections based on URL-driven state.
event_handlers: onContentRender: After rendering sections for the active document, ensures that each section is assigned a stable anchor or identifier derived from its index or heading.
onActiveSectionChange: When activeSection changes, scrolls to the matching section anchor and may set keyboard focus to the section heading for accessibility.

onInDocumentLinkClick: Handles clicks on in-document anchor links (for example, links with href values referencing section anchors); updates the activeSection and section query parameter to match the anchor.
onExternalLinkClick: Allows links in body_html that point outside the current page to follow standard navigation behavior without altering internal state.

components.functionality: Shows document-specific error messages and retry options when loading either the privacy policy or terms of service fails.
event_handlers: onRetryPrivacyClick: When the user clicks retry for the privacy policy, calls policy-page-loader.onReloadPrivacy to invoke getPrivacyPolicyContent again.
onRetryTermsClick: When the user clicks retry for the terms of service, calls policy-page-loader.onReloadTerms to invoke getTermsOfServiceContent again.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column centered grid with a max-width content container. The two-column content section appears roughly 6/6 or 7/5 split between text and image.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a slim yellow utility bar at the very top, the main white header with logo and navigation beneath it, followed by a full-width hero banner with a workshop/building image and a left-aligned 'Privacy & Terms' heading. Directly beneath (and slightly overlapping) the bottom edge of the hero, the main policy area appears as a two-column layout within a centered max-width container. The left column holds the functional controls and navigation for the policies (tabs, last-updated, table of contents), and the right column holds the document viewer itself. System components like the loader and state manager operate within this structure but don’t break the linear flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the same top-to-bottom structure as the mockup: a slim yellow utility bar at the very top, the main white header with logo and navigation beneath it, followed by a full-width hero banner with a workshop/building image and a left-aligned 'Privacy & Terms' heading. Directly beneath (and slightly overlapping) the bottom edge of the hero, the main policy area appears as a two-column layout within a centered max-width container. The left column holds the functional controls and navigation for the policies (tabs, last-updated, table of contents), and the right column holds the document viewer itself. System components like the loader and state manager operate within this structure but don’t break the linear flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The utility bar and header together consume roughly the top 10–15% of the viewport height. The hero banner occupies about 25–30% of the viewport on initial load, with generous lateral margins defined by a centered content container up to ~1200px wide. The overlapping policy content block fills the remaining initial viewport height, taking up about 65–75% of the width of the centered container for the right document panel and 25–35% for the left controls panel (approximately a 5/12 and 7/12 split on a 12-column grid). Vertical gaps between header, hero, and content are in the 24–32px range, with the overlapping panels using negative margin to minimize empty space.
layout_strategies.content_density: The content density remains moderate and brochure-like. The policy text is long-form but set in a comfortably padded, scrollable panel with 16–24px internal padding and generous line height. The left column’s tabs, last-updated banner, and table of contents are stacked with clear spacing between groups, avoiding clutter despite multiple functional elements. No cramped multi-column text blocks; instead, a single main reading column on the right is balanced by navigational elements on the left.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the original F-pattern. The eye starts at the top-left logo and runs across the yellow utility bar and main navigation. It then drops down to the hero’s left-aligned 'Privacy & Terms' heading, and from there moves directly into the overlapping left policy panel where the active tab label and last-updated info sit high in the hierarchy. The reader then scans down the table of contents list before moving horizontally to the right to read the policy text. The right-hand document viewer is the main reading area and is revisited repeatedly, while status or error messages appear near the top of this right panel so they are seen immediately after the user’s initial sweep down from the hero heading.

layout.overall_description: Below the familiar yellow utility bar and clean white header with centered navigation, a full-width hero image of the workshop or building stretches across the page. On the left middle of this hero, a bold white 'Privacy & Terms' title anchors the page and visually ties into the automotive brand’s blue-and-yellow palette. As the hero image ends, the main policy content rises up to overlap its lower edge: on the left, a semi-opaque, light panel about one-third of the content width floats over the background, containing the document tabs, a subtle last-updated strip, and a vertically stacked table of contents. On the right, occupying roughly two-thirds of the width, a solid white document viewer panel extends slightly higher and lower than the left panel, giving it dominance as the main reading zone while still fitting within the same grid. Both panels sit within a centered max-width container with generous side margins, and the entire composition feels like a brochure laid over a workshop photo. As the user scrolls, the header remains at the top of the page, while the left navigation column and right content column move together, keeping the relationship between navigation and document clear and consistent with the original two-column service layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader is a subtle, unobtrusive layer associated primarily with the main policy content area rather than the entire viewport. Visually, when active, it appears as a thin horizontal loading bar or narrow status strip aligned to the top edge of the right-hand document viewer panel, spanning roughly 80–100% of that panel’s width. It sits flush against the inner top border of the document viewer, maintaining the clean rectangular geometry. Alternatively, for blocking states, a soft, semi-transparent white veil can cover just the document viewer panel (and optionally dim the left panel slightly) while a small spinner is centered in the upper third of the right panel. It never interferes with the header, hero, or global layout; instead, it is visually anchored to the overlapping two-column policy area, emphasizing that the document content is loading while the rest of the page remains stable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the policy content region; overlays only the document viewer (and optionally lightly tints the left navigation panel) while active. It scrolls with the page, appearing only when data is in a loading state and disappearing without shifting surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The state manager has no direct visual footprint. Conceptually, it sits behind the entire hero and two-column content region, coordinating which document (privacy policy or terms of service) is displayed in the right-hand viewer and which tab appears active in the left panel. It affects visual elements such as the selected tab styling, highlighted table-of-contents items, and any error or loading messages, but it does not introduce any visible block, border, or spacing of its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Purely logical; no visible position. It influences the visual state of other components but does not occupy space or alter the structural layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tab switcher is positioned near the top of the left overlapping panel, directly below the bottom edge of the hero image and aligned to the left margin of the main content container. It spans roughly 80–90% of the width of this left panel, leaving comfortable horizontal padding on both sides (about 16–24px). The tabs are laid out horizontally—typically two options such as 'Privacy Policy' and 'Terms of Service'—with the active tab styled in the brand’s blue and sitting slightly in front visually, echoing the high-contrast bar patterns from the mockup. Vertical space above the tabs is minimal (8–16px) to preserve the sense that they are tightly linked to the hero title, while a 16–24px gap below them leads into the last-updated banner. Overall, the tab switcher visually anchors the top of the left column and invites the first interaction after the hero heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column; it scrolls off-screen with the rest of the content on smaller viewports. On taller screens, it remains at the top of the left panel, with no fixed or sticky behavior by default.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The last-updated banner sits immediately beneath the tab switcher within the same left overlapping panel, maintaining the vertical stacking pattern. It is a slim, full-width strip inside the left column card, about 32–40px tall, spanning nearly the entire width of that card (minus internal padding). It may use a very light tint of the brand’s yellow or a subtle grey background, with small, left-aligned text such as 'Last updated: DD Month YYYY' and possibly a tiny clock icon aligned to the left, reinforcing the utilitarian feel of the top utility bar. There is a 8–12px vertical gap above and below this banner to keep it visually distinct from the tabs and the table of contents list, yet compact enough not to dominate the left panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the left column, scrolling with the page. It always stays just below the tab switcher and above the table of contents, preserving that order regardless of screen height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The table of contents occupies the remainder of the left overlapping panel beneath the last-updated banner. It is a vertically scrolling list contained within the left column’s card-like background, starting about 16–24px below the banner. The TOC spans the full usable width of the left panel (again with 16–24px side padding) and extends downward for the full height of the content region, typically covering roughly half to two-thirds of the visible height of the document viewer on initial load. Each TOC entry is a single-line or two-line item with moderate vertical spacing (8–12px between items) to maintain the mockup’s readable density. More important sections can be slightly indented or bolded, but the list remains simple and practical, echoing the straightforward text presentation used in the 'Logbook Servicing' content. As the page scrolls, this TOC scrolls along naturally; on large screens, it may appear to sit almost entirely within one viewport height beside the document text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left column, scrolling as part of the page. It does not fix or pin itself, but its vertical extent can overflow and scroll if there are many sections, using the page scroll rather than its own internal scrollbar where possible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The document viewer is the dominant panel on the right side of the overlapping content area. It aligns with the right edge of the main centered container and spans approximately 60–65% of the container’s width (about 7 of 12 columns), leaving the remaining 35–40% to the left overlapping panel. Vertically, its top edge overlaps the bottom of the hero slightly more than the left panel does, giving it a slight visual precedence. The viewer itself is a solid white, rectangular block with subtle shadow or border, clearly distinguished from any underlying workshop imagery. Inside, ample padding (16–24px on all sides) frames the policy text, which flows in a single-column format with comfortable line height. Section headings within the document align with the entries listed in the TOC, creating a clear connection. On larger screens, the initial viewport typically shows the document title, the first section heading or two, and the beginning of the body text. The panel is vertically scrollable with the rest of the page; the background stays fixed while the document content moves, maintaining the clean brochure-like structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow, occupying the right segment of the two-column layout. It scrolls with the page and does not fix or float, ensuring a simple, predictable reading experience that aligns with traditional long-form pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notice appears within the right-hand document viewer panel so that any problems loading a policy are communicated directly where the user expects to see content. When active, it occupies a horizontal strip near the top of the document viewer’s interior, pushing the regular policy text down. This strip spans the full inner width of the viewer (inside the panel’s padding) and is about 56–72px tall, depending on the length of the message. It uses a clear, high-contrast style—such as a soft red or amber background with dark text—while still fitting the site’s modest corporate look. An icon (e.g., a small warning triangle) sits on the left, with the message text to the right and possibly a link or button to retry loading. The entire notice is separated from the top border and subsequent content by 12–16px of vertical space, ensuring it is noticeable but not overwhelming. When no error is present, this space collapses and the document content shifts up to reclaim the area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the document viewer, appearing and disappearing without changing the outer dimensions of the panel. It scrolls with the policy text; if an error occurs mid-scroll, the user will see it as part of the current scroll position, but initial load errors appear near the top of the panel so they are immediately visible.
